feature: BYO model registry injection into openclaw.json
expected_behaviour: `_byo_model_extras(tenant)` in config_generator returns an empty dict if `tenant.byo_models_enabled` is False or no BYO credential for (anthropic, cli_subscription) exists with a status other than ERROR. When a valid credential exists, returns `{'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6': {'alias': 'claude-sonnet'}, 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-5': {'alias': 'claude-opus'}}`. These are merged into the tenant's model allowlist by `resolve_tenant_models`. When the resolved primary model is a BYO model, `fallbacks_list` is explicitly set to `[]` so billing/auth errors surface instead of silently switching to a platform-metered model. `generate_openclaw_config` also adds `cliBackends.claude-cli.command=/opt/nbhd/claude-with-token.sh` when BYO Anthropic Sonnet is in extras.
original_claim: The expected behaviour states BYO extras include `anthropic/claude-opus-4-5` but the code serves `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` (via `ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL` constant). The spec is stale — the code docstring, constant, and implementation all agree on opus-4-7. Subscribers see the correct (newer) model but the inventory description is wrong, which creates a documentation gap if the spec is used to write integration tests.
refutation: Not a product defect. The discrepancy is entirely between the audit spec's hardcoded expected_behaviour string (claude-opus-4-5) and the actual code, which consistently uses anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 across all layers: the constant ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL (constants.py:75), the display name ANTHROPIC_OPUS_DISPLAY = "Claude Opus 4.7" (constants.py:76), the _byo_model_extras docstring "Claude Opus 4.7" (config_generator.py:983), and the implementation (config_generator.py:1009 maps _OPUS_MODEL to alias "claude-opus"). The code is internally self-consistent and serves the newer/correct model. A subscriber's openclaw.json receives the right model id and routes correctly through the anthropic:claude-cli auth profile — zero runtime impact. The QA verdict itself concedes subscribers see the correct model and that only the inventory description is wrong. The stale artifact is the QA spec JSON (docs/feature-audit/areas/byo_models__1.json), a generated audit document, not shipped code or user-facing docs. A spec lagging behind the implementation is not a data-integrity defect in the feature; the hypothetical "documentation gap if the spec is used to write integration tests" applies to the audit artifact, not the product.
refutation_evidence: apps/billing/constants.py:72-76 — ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL = 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-7', ANTHROPIC_OPUS_DISPLAY = 'Claude Opus 4.7'
apps/orchestrator/config_generator.py:983-988 — docstring says 'Claude Opus 4.7' + late import of ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL as _OPUS_MODEL
apps/orchestrator/config_generator.py:1007-1009 — extras[_OPUS_MODEL] = {'alias': 'claude-opus'} under has_anthropic_cli

expected_behaviour: Django admin at /admin/core/meditationsession/. list_display: title, tenant, date, status, voice, duration_ms, created_at. list_filter: status. search_fields: title, theme, tenant__user__display_name. readonly_fields: id, created_at, updated_at. The error field is visible for failed sessions to expose the last render error message.
original_claim: The MeditationSessionAdmin does not include the `error` field in `readonly_fields`, so it renders as an editable textarea in the change form. The expected behaviour states the error field should be visible for diagnosing failed sessions, implying read-only audit access; an admin could accidentally overwrite or clear the last render error message, destroying diagnostic data.
refutation: Not a defect. The claim is factually correct that `error` is absent from `readonly_fields` (apps/core/admin.py:19) and therefore renders as an editable textarea. But the feature's own expected_behaviour explicitly specifies "readonly_fields: id, created_at, updated_at" and separately says only that "The error field is visible for failed sessions to expose the last render error message." An editable field IS visible in the Django admin change form, so the implementation exactly matches the documented spec. The QA verdict invents a stricter "read-only audit access" requirement by inferring it from the word "visible" — the spec never asks for `error` to be read-only. Additionally, this surface is the Django admin, accessible only to trusted staff/operators (user_role is "admin/operator", not an end-user); editing the diagnostic `error` field would require deliberately typing into the textarea and clicking Save, which is ordinary Django admin behaviour for essentially every non-timestamp field on every registered model (CoreProfileAdmin uses the same readonly set). The error data is overwritten by the render pipeline on each attempt (help_text: "Last render error, when status=failed"), not a permanent audit artifact requiring write protection. Intended/acceptable behaviour matching the spec.
refutation_evidence: apps/core/admin.py:14-19 — MeditationSessionAdmin readonly_fields = [id, created_at, updated_at]; error not included
apps/core/models.py:126 — error = TextField(blank=True, default="", help_text="Last render error, when status=failed")

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/cron-jobs/ (tenant-scoped). Requires DRF IsAuthenticated. If tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=True, reads CronJob table directly (no gateway call). If tenant is hibernated, serves from Postgres cache. If gateway is reachable, calls cron.list on the OpenClaw container, upserts results into the CronJob cache, and updates Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot. Filters out hidden system crons (Background Tasks, Heartbeat Check-in, Personal Question, Project Check-in) and names starting with _sync:/ _fuel: prefixes. Each visible job is tagged with foreground boolean based on presence of Phase 2 sync marker in payload.message. Returns {jobs: [...]} with 200. Returns 502 if gateway is unreachable and no cache exists.
original_claim: The expected behaviour states the endpoint returns 502 when the gateway is unreachable and no cache exists. In the legacy (non-pg-canonical) path, `read_jobs_from_cache` returns an empty list when the CronJob table is empty and `cron_jobs_snapshot` is absent, so the view returns 200 with `{"jobs": []}` rather than 502. A subscriber with no previous successful cron.list sync will see a silently empty Scheduled Tasks card instead of an error banner when the container is down.
refutation: False positive on two independent grounds. (1) The cited code path is effectively dead for the entire fleet. The empty-cache-instead-of-502 case only exists in the legacy branch reached when tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=False (tenant_views.py:236-269). Migration 0058 set this flag True for all 24 existing tenants and migration 0067 made True the model default for all new tenants (0067 docstring: "all 24 already True via 0058"). Every request short-circuits at tenant_views.py:223 to postgres_canonical.list_visible_jobs, which reads the CronJob table directly and never calls the gateway, so the container being unreachable is irrelevant on the live path and no production subscriber can reach the branch the claim describes. (2) Even within that legacy branch, returning 200 {jobs: []} on container-unavailable is intentional graceful degradation, not a defect. Comments at tenant_views.py:228-235 explicitly distinguish "no active container at all" (_require_active_tenant raises GatewayError -> 502 at lines 237-239) from "container provisioned but momentarily unreachable" (cold start / transient Azure ingress 404/502/503/504/timeout -> serve cache at lines 244-249, is_container_unavailable_error in cache.py:34-47). The cache-fallback was purpose-built to AVOID surfacing the confusing Azure "Container App - Unavailable" splash to users; an empty cache during the brief pre-first-sync window is an accepted edge of that design. The expected_behaviour clause "Returns 502 if gateway is unreachable and no cache exists" is the auditor's own reconstruction and conflates the deliberate two-tier handling; the genuine 502 (no active container) is preserved. The frontend does render distinct states (rose error banner on 502 at page.tsx:710-719 vs "No scheduled tasks configured yet." on empty 200 at page.tsx:1132), but the empty-200 state is unreachable in production and intended where it exists.

apps/cron/tenant_views.py:228-235 (comments: hibernated->cache, not-provisioned->502, fallback only for container-unavailable)

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/bulk-delete/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Accepts {ids: [name-or-id, ...]}. Returns 400 if ids is not a non-empty list. Returns 403 if any id is a hidden system cron. Deduplicates ids preserving order. If postgres_cron_canonical: deletes rows in atomic transaction, signal fires per-row. Legacy: if tenant is hibernated, wakes container and returns 503 with container_waking=true. Iterates gateway cron.remove calls; if mid-loop unavailability detected, aborts and returns 503. Returns 200 on full success, 207 on partial success, 502 if all fail.
original_claim: The expected behaviour says the endpoint returns 502 when all bulk-delete calls fail. In the postgres-canonical path (`bulk_delete_jobs`), when every requested job is not found the function returns `HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND`, not 502. The legacy gateway path correctly returns 502 when all `cron.remove` calls fail. A frontend coded to treat 502 as the all-failed signal will silently mishandle a 404 on the pg path, leaving the user with no feedback that deletion failed.
refutation: The status-code divergence is real in code -- the pg-canonical path (postgres_canonical.py:265-267) returns 404 when every job is not-found, while the legacy gateway path (tenant_views.py:754-756) returns 502 when all cron.remove calls fail -- but the claimed USER IMPACT does not exist. The claim hinges on 'a frontend coded to treat 502 as the all-failed signal,' which the actual frontend is NOT. The only consumer is the web dashboard: bulkDeleteCronJobs() (frontend/lib/api.ts:1030) calls apiFetch, which throws on ANY non-2xx response (api.ts:171, 'if (!response.ok)'), attaching the status but never branching on it. handleBulkDeleteConfirm (cron-jobs/page.tsx:494-507) wraps the call in try/catch and on any thrown error calls setBulkDeleteError(getErrorMessage(err)), rendering a visible rose-colored error message (page.tsx:770-771). So 404 and 502 are handled identically -- both surface error feedback to the user; neither is silently mishandled. There is no 502-specific branch anywhere in the frontend, and no iOS/Swift consumer of this endpoint exists. Additionally the 404 is arguably more correct than 502 for the pg path: 'all rows not found' is a genuine not-found condition with no gateway involved, whereas 502 (Bad Gateway) describes the legacy path's gateway-failure semantics. The two paths legitimately differ because their failure modes differ. The spec line '502 if all fail' describes legacy gateway behaviour; it is not a contract the frontend depends on. No real user or operator experiences a defect.
refutation_evidence: apps/cron/postgres_canonical.py:253-276 (bulk_delete_jobs returns 404 when errors and not results, 207 partial, 200 full)
apps/cron/tenant_views.py:706-711 (pg path dispatch returns result _status)
apps/cron/tenant_views.py:754-758 (legacy returns 502 when errors and not results)
frontend/lib/api.ts:1024-1035 (bulkDeleteCronJobs / BulkDeleteResult)
frontend/lib/api.ts:171-176 (apiFetch throws on any !response.ok; no per-status branching)
frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:494-507 (try/catch surfaces error via setBulkDeleteError on any thrown status)
frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:770-771 (visible error rendering)

feature: Cron job bulk foreground/background toggle (subscriber dashboard)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/bulk-update-foreground/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Accepts {ids: [name-or-id, ...], foreground: bool}. Returns 400 if ids invalid or foreground not a boolean. Returns 403 if any id is a hidden system cron. If postgres_cron_canonical: strips existing Phase 2 block, re-wraps per foreground flag, saves each row, signal fires. Legacy path: fetches all jobs, rebuilds each job with delete+recreate, rolls back on failure. Skips jobs already in the desired state. Returns 200, 207 (partial), or 502.
original_claim: The expected behaviour says the endpoint returns 502 when all updates fail. In the postgres-canonical path (`bulk_update_foreground`), when all jobs either do not exist or have no payload, the function returns `HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST` rather than 502. The legacy gateway path returns 502 when all `cron.remove`/`cron.add` calls fail. The inconsistent error code between the two paths may confuse the frontend error-handling layer.
refutation: The code facts are accurate: postgres_canonical.bulk_update_foreground (postgres_canonical.py:333-335) returns HTTP 400 on the all-fail case, while the legacy gateway path (tenant_views.py:918-920) returns HTTP 502. But this is not a real defect. (1) The differing codes are semantically defensible: the Postgres path's failures are purely local data conditions (job name not found, or payload not a dict) with NO upstream gateway call, so 400 (bad/unprocessable request) is correct; the legacy path's all-fail case is dominated by GatewayError (the OpenClaw container failing cron.remove/cron.add), so 502 (bad gateway) is correct. The only scenario where identical input yields different codes is the corner case 'all requested job IDs do not exist'. (2) The claimed impact -- 'may confuse the frontend error-handling layer' -- is unsubstantiated: the sole frontend caller (frontend/lib/queries.ts:1048-1060 useBulkUpdateForegroundMutation) handles onError generically by invalidating the cron-jobs cache and never branches on HTTP status; apiFetch (frontend/lib/api.ts:98) has no 400/502/207 special-casing either. No code path distinguishes the two responses. (3) Normal UX toggles jobs the dashboard already lists (which exist), so updates succeed (200/207); the all-fail branch is effectively unreachable in practice. (4) The legacy path is deprecated -- postgres_cron_canonical is the canonical default (model default flipped True per migration 0067), so active tenants take the Postgres path. Cosmetic API-semantics nit in a deprecated corner case with no demonstrated user/operator impact.
refutation_evidence: apps/cron/postgres_canonical.py:314-344 (errors populated only by local 'not found'/'no payload' conditions, no gateway call; 400 when errors and not results)
apps/cron/tenant_views.py:859-931 (legacy path; errors from GatewayError on cron.remove/add and 'Job not found' at :870; 502 when errors and not results)
apps/cron/tenant_views.py:819-826 (postgres_cron_canonical dispatch — canonical default path)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:1048-1060 (useBulkUpdateForegroundMutation onError just invalidates cache, no status branching)
frontend/lib/api.ts:98 (apiFetch has no 400/502/207 special-casing)

feature: Register system QStash cron schedules (CI deploy hook)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/cron/register-system-crons/. Auth: X-Deploy-Secret header (401 if wrong, 400 if base_url missing, 503 if QSTASH_TOKEN not configured). Fetches existing QStash schedules via API. For each entry in SYSTEM_CRONS (25 schedules): skips if destination+cron_expr unchanged, deletes+recreates if cron expression changed, creates if new. Returns {registered, updated, skipped, failed} lists.
original_claim: The expected behaviour states SYSTEM_CRONS contains 25 schedules. The actual list in the management command and the view both contain exactly 24 entries. This is a documentation drift rather than a code bug, but if any monitoring or CI assertion counts against the stated 25, it would false-positive.
refutation: Not a defect. SYSTEM_CRONS contains exactly 24 tuple entries (verified by listing every entry at register_system_crons.py:21-110). The "25" the QA flags exists ONLY in the feature-audit record's own auditor-written expected_behaviour and inventory_notes; it is not asserted anywhere in code, tests, or monitoring. The view at views.py:935 imports the very same SYSTEM_CRONS list and iterates it at views.py:957 (for name, cron_expr, path in SYSTEM_CRONS), so the management command and the view are one shared source of truth, not two divergent lists. The register loop is count-agnostic: it processes whatever the list holds, so 24 vs 25 has zero runtime impact; all crons register/update/skip correctly. A repo-wide search found no test or CI assertion comparing the list length to 25 (the only "25" matches are cron-minute fields like "25 * * * *" and an unrelated canary Apr-25 date test). The QA's own escape hatch ("IF any monitoring or CI assertion counts against the stated 25") is unfulfilled; no such guard exists. This is documentation drift in the audit artifact itself, affecting no user or operator.

apps/cron/views.py:935 (imports SYSTEM_CRONS from the management command)

feature: Backfill Telegram delivery targets management command
expected_behaviour: python manage.py backfill_cron_targets. Targets active tenants with a container FQDN and a telegram_chat_id. For each: fetches cron.list, finds jobs with delivery.mode=announce, delivery.channel=telegram, and no delivery.to set. Updates each such job via cron.update with delivery.to=str(telegram_chat_id). Prints per-job/per-tenant results. Skips tenants with gateway errors.
original_claim: The backfill command's purpose is defeated by the iOS delivery normalization backstop in `invoke_gateway_tool`. When it calls `cron.update` with a patch containing `delivery: {mode: 'announce', channel: 'telegram', to: '<chat_id>'}`, the normalization at gateway_client.py:154-166 intercepts the call, detects that the delivery is not iOS-safe, and silently rewrites the patch to `delivery: {mode: 'none'}` before sending to the gateway. The command logs a success and increments its counter, but the actual delivery written to the gateway discards the `to` field and switches the mode from `announce` to `none`, so Telegram delivery is broken rather than fixed for those jobs.
refutation: The interception mechanism is real, but the claimed harm ("Telegram delivery is broken rather than fixed") misreads intent, making this a false positive. Chronology: backfill_cron_targets.py (2026-02-19, commit 4116937) was a ONE-TIME migration to populate delivery.to on legacy announce/telegram crons. The iOS backstop (_normalize_cron_delivery_for_ios, 2026-06-21, commit 7e40ef0) was added 4 months later and DELIBERATELY rewrites any non-iOS-safe delivery patch to {mode:"none"} at gateway_client.py:159-166. So yes, the backfill's cron.update patch ({delivery:{mode:announce,channel:telegram,to:chat_id}}) does get rewritten to {mode:"none"}, discarding to/channel. BUT: (1) the announce/telegram path the backfill targets is already broken fleet-wide per the backstop commit ("broken on this fleet anyway: no bot token at the OC channel layer") and never reaches iOS — so the backstop is NOT breaking working delivery; it routes to the CORRECT mode:none + nbhd_send_to_user path that actually reaches the user via Django push. (2) The backstop commit states "Production currently has zero bypassing user crons (all 26 are mode:none)", so the backfill's filter (mode=announce, channel=telegram, no to) matches no jobs in production — the command has no rows to act on. The backfill is a superseded, idle artifact, not a live conflict causing user harm. The only genuine residual is cosmetic: a matching job would log "delivery.to = chat_id" and increment the success counter (lines 78-82) while the gateway actually received {mode:"none"} — misleading operator logging. That is a trivial observability nit in an obsolete one-time command, not the medium-severity broken-Telegram-delivery defect claimed; rewriting to mode:none is the intended, desirable end state.
refutation_evidence: apps/cron/management/commands/backfill_cron_targets.py:50-82 (filters announce/telegram/no-to, then cron.update with delivery.to, logs success unconditionally at 78-82)
apps/cron/gateway_client.py:159-166 (cron.update patch path rewrites non-iOS-safe delivery to {mode:none}, dropping to/channel)
apps/cron/gateway_client.py:36-39,42-85 (_is_ios_safe_delivery / _normalize_cron_delivery_for_ios: only mode:none-no-channel is iOS-safe)
git log 4116937 (2026-02-19 backfill command created) vs 7e40ef0 (2026-06-21 iOS backstop added 4mo later)
commit 7e40ef0 message: announce/telegram path 'broken on this fleet anyway: no bot token at the OC channel layer'; 'Production currently has zero bypassing user crons (all 26 are mode:none)'

expected_behaviour: SYSTEM_CRONS is a list of (name, cron_expr, path) tuples. Covers 25 schedules including: apply-pending-configs (hourly), reset-daily-counters (daily midnight), reset-monthly-counters (monthly), expire-trials (daily 02:00), hibernate-idle-tenants (hourly), reconcile-tenant-crons (hourly :05), reconcile-fuel-crons (hourly), ensure-at-cron-wakes (every 5 min), nightly-extraction (hourly), pii-arbiter (hourly :40), reap-stuck-inbound-messages (every minute), snapshot-gravity-weekly (Sunday 05:00), model-health-check (every 30 min), and more. All cron_exprs are UTC.
original_claim: The expected behaviour states the SYSTEM_CRONS list covers '25 schedules', but the actual list contains 24 entries. This is a documentation/inventory discrepancy rather than a runtime bug, but it means any tooling or documentation that relies on that count will be off by one.
refutation: Not a defect. The "25 schedules" count exists ONLY in the audit's own generated artifacts (docs/feature-audit/features_raw.json, areas/cron__3.json, FEATURE_AUDIT.csv) — it is the auditor's own estimate of the list size, not a value present anywhere in the application source. The actual SYSTEM_CRONS list contains 24 (name, cron_expr, path) tuples. The command does NOT reference any hardcoded count: it iterates `for name, cron_expr, path in SYSTEM_CRONS:` and tallies registered/updated/skipped dynamically. No runtime code, CI step, or operator tooling depends on a literal "25", so there is nothing to be "off by one." The claimed impact ("any tooling or documentation that relies on that count will be off by one") is hypothetical and unfounded — the only document naming 25 is the audit's own expected_behaviour text, which miscounted. A real operator running register_system_crons sees the correct 24 schedules registered with no discrepancy. This is a self-referential error in the audit artifact, not a product defect.

feature: Horizons Aggregation (Goals + Mood + Momentum + Insights + Topic Signals)
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/dashboard/horizons/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns HTTP 404 if no tenant. On success (HTTP 200) returns a JSON object with seven sections: (1) "goals" — union of typed Goal (status=ACTIVE) and legacy Document(kind=GOAL) rows, deduplicated via Goal.migrated_from_document; returns up to 20 items sorted by -updated_at, each with id, title, slug ("typed:<uuid>" for typed Goals), preview (first 200 chars of markdown), created_at, updated_at; inactive typed goals (ACHIEVED/ABANDONED) are excluded. (2) "pending_extractions" — up to 10 non-expired PendingExtraction rows of kind GOAL or TASK with status PENDING, ordered by -created_at. (3) "weekly_pulse" — up to 4 WeeklyReview rows (legacy model), ordered by -week_start, each with week_start, week_end, week_rating, top_win (first element of top_wins or null). (4) "weekly_documents" — up to 4 Document(kind=WEEKLY) rows, serialized via _serialize_weekly_document() which derives week_start/week_end from YYYY-MM-DD slug, strips markdown to a 180-char plain text preview, and includes full markdown. (5) "mood_trend" — all JournalEntry rows in the last 30 days with date, mood, energy fields. (6) "momentum" — 30-day array (today - 29d to today) where each entry has date, message_count (UsageRecord count for that date), has_journal (True if JournalEntry OR Document(kind=DAILY) exists for that date); plus "current_streak" (consecutive days with activity counting backwards from today). (7) "assistant_insights" — up to 20 AssistantInsight rows with status OPEN or CONFIRMED (REFUTED excluded), ordered by -created_at, with id, pillar, topic_slug, topic_display_name, statement, status, confidence, created_at, last_confirmed_at. (8) "topic_signals" — per-topic meta-state from apps/insights/signals.summarize_topic_signals(), covering topics with any engagement (snapshots, insights, goals, or voice prefs), returning sample_size, confirmed/refuted counts, has_goal flag, register_offset/register_scope. Response is cached per-tenant with a 60-second TTL under the "dashboard" cache tag; cache bypassed if Redis tag_v == 0.
original_claim: The deduplication of migrated legacy goal documents fetches migrated_doc_ids from the full unsliced typed_goals_qs, then slices typed_goals to :20. If more than 20 typed Goals exist, goals ranked 21+ that have migrated_from_document set cause their corresponding legacy Document rows to be excluded from legacy_goals — even though the typed Goal itself does not appear in the typed_goals list due to the :20 slice. This can silently drop a goal from the combined union entirely. In practice this requires >20 active typed Goals per tenant, which is rare but possible.
refutation: False positive. migrated_doc_ids is built from the FULL unsliced typed_goals_qs while typed_goals is sliced to [:20]. The claim is that a typed Goal ranked 21+ with migrated_from_document set would exclude its legacy Document yet not itself appear, silently dropping a goal. But that exclusion can never affect the visible result, which is itself capped at 20 sorted by -updated_at per the feature contract. The migration command creates the typed Goal via Goal.objects.create(...) WITHOUT setting updated_at, and Goal.updated_at uses auto_now=True, so the typed Goal G gets the migration timestamp, always >= its source Document D's updated_at (later edits only push G more recent). For G to rank 21+, there must be >=20 typed goals with updated_at >= G.updated_at >= D.updated_at, all of which also outrank D; therefore D could never have placed in the combined top-20 anyway, so excluding it loses nothing visible. Excluding ALL migrated legacy docs regardless of slice position is in fact the intended dedup behavior: a legacy doc folded into a typed Goal should never render as a separate row. The only way D could outrank G is a post-migration independent edit to the legacy Document, which is not an expected flow (the dual-read exists precisely to phase legacy docs out), and even then suppressing the stale legacy copy in favor of the typed Goal is correct, not a bug. The trigger also requires >20 active typed goals per tenant, which is rare. No real user-visible defect.
refutation_evidence: apps/dashboard/views.py:211-249 (migrated_doc_ids from unsliced qs, typed_goals[:20], legacy exclude+[:20], final sorted[:20])
apps/journal/models.py:314-326 (updated_at auto_now=True; ordering -updated_at)
apps/journal/management/commands/migrate_documents_to_typed_models.py:86-98 (Goal.objects.create does not set updated_at, so typed Goal carries migration-time timestamp >= source Document)
docs/feature-audit/areas/dashboard.json:33 (contract: returns up to 20 items sorted by -updated_at)

expected_behaviour: Each reminder row has a 'Cancel' button. Clicking it shows an inline confirmation: 'Confirm cancel' (rose) and 'Keep' buttons. A 3-second auto-timeout (CANCEL_CONFIRM_TIMEOUT_MS) reverts to the initial Cancel button if no action is taken. Clicking 'Confirm cancel' fires cancelMutation.mutate(job.name), calling DELETE /api/v1/cron-jobs/pending-at/{name}/. While pending, the button shows 'Cancelling…' and is disabled. On success, the ['pending-reminders'] query is invalidated and the reminder disappears. On error, a rose error message appears inline under the reminder name.
original_claim: The cancel mutation does not invalidate the ['pending-reminders'] query on error — if the DELETE call fails, the reminder stays in the list but no error feedback is shown because the 'Cancelling...' button text reverts to 'Cancel' without explanation. The user sees no indication that the cancel failed; they have to retry manually without knowing the first attempt failed.
refutation: False positive — a misread. The claim asserts that on a failed cancel DELETE "no error feedback is shown" and "the user sees no indication the cancel failed." The code contradicts this. While the mutation hook (queries.ts:1077-1085) has no onError, the component supplies an onError at the call site: handleCancelConfirm (pending-reminders-section.tsx:137-147) calls cancelMutation.mutate(job.name, { onError: ... }) which writes the error into per-job cancelError state ("Cancel failed." or the error's message). That message is then rendered in rose/red text under the reminder at lines 190-192. So a failed cancel DOES show explicit, inline, per-reminder error feedback. The reminder correctly remaining in the list is right (cancellation did not happen), and not invalidating ['pending-reminders'] on error is correct — a refetch would just return the same unchanged list. The reviewer overlooked the call-site onError handler and the cancelError render block.
refutation_evidence: frontend/components/pending-reminders-section.tsx:137-147 (mutate with onError writing cancelError)
frontend/components/pending-reminders-section.tsx:190-192 (renders cancelError in rose text under the reminder)
frontend/components/pending-reminders-section.tsx:91 (cancelError state declaration)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:1077-1085 (mutation has onSuccess-only, no onError invalidation)

expected_behaviour: The useToast hook provides a [toast, showToast] pair. showToast(message, type) creates a toast with a unique id. The Toast component renders conditionally when toast is non-null at the top of the page. Toasts are used for: bulk delete success ('N tasks deleted', 'success'), bulk foreground update success, and error toasts from bulk foreground mutation errors. The toast auto-dismisses via the hook's internal mechanism. Per the component signature, the onDismiss callback is a no-op in the page (the hook manages state).
original_claim: The Toast rendered in the cron-jobs page is never removed from the DOM after it animates out because the onDismiss callback is a no-op and the useToast hook has no internal auto-dismiss timer. The Toast component sets itself to opacity-0/pointer-events-none after durationMs-300ms and calls onDismiss() after durationMs, but the page's onDismiss does nothing, so the hook's toast state stays non-null. The ghost Toast element (with aria-live='polite') remains mounted permanently, meaning screen readers see a stale live region indefinitely. Sighted users are unaffected for the first toast, but the mismatch between the code comment ('state auto-managed by hook') and the actual hook implementation (no auto-clear) is a latent bug for any future code that checks toast != null.
refutation: Not a user-facing defect. The mechanical facts are accurate: the cron-jobs page passes a no-op onDismiss (page.tsx:533, comment "state auto-managed by hook") while useToast (toast.tsx:94-103) has no auto-clear timer, so the hook's toast state stays non-null and the Toast div remains mounted (in opacity-0/pointer-events-none state) after it fades. But no real user experiences this as a defect. (1) Sighted users are fully unaffected: the lingering div is invisible and non-interactive, and every subsequent showToast() bumps _toastCounter giving a new key={toast.id}, so React remounts the Toast and re-runs the in/out animation; all toasts render correctly. (2) The screen-reader claim misreads ARIA semantics: an aria-live=polite region announces content CHANGES, not its mere presence. A silent, idle live region persisting in the DOM is standard practice in virtually every toast library and produces no spurious/stale announcements; when the next toast fires, the key change unmounts the old node and mounts a fresh one with new text, which announces correctly. There is no harmful 'stale live region the SR sees indefinitely.' (3) The 'latent bug for future code that checks toast != null' is speculative: no current code reads toast except to render it. The no-op onDismiss plus misleading comment is a genuine code-cleanliness smell (GlobalToastHost at toast.tsx:111-124 shows the intended onDismiss={() => setToast(null)} pattern), but it yields zero observable defect for any user, sighted or assistive-tech. Fails the 'would a real user actually experience this' bar.

frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:528-535

feature: Constellation Graph — Delete Lesson from Graph
expected_behaviour: The Inspector shows a 'Remove from constellation' button only for Lesson nodes. Clicking it reveals a confirmation row with a 'Delete' button and a 'Cancel' button. Clicking 'Delete' calls useDeleteLessonMutation which sends DELETE /api/v1/lessons/{id}/. While in-flight both buttons are disabled and 'Delete' shows 'Deleting...'. On success, the mutation invalidates the ['constellation'] and ['pending-lessons'] query caches, which refetches and re-renders the graph without the deleted node. The Inspector closes (setSelectedId(null)). On error, the mutation raises and the buttons re-enable (but the error is not surfaced in the UI from what is visible in the code).
original_claim: On delete mutation failure, no error message is surfaced to the user. The `onDelete` call passes only an `onSuccess` handler (`deleteLesson.mutate(id, { onSuccess: () => setSelectedId(null) })`); there is no `onError` handler and no error state is read from `deleteLesson`. The Inspector stays open with the Delete button re-enabled (via `deleting` returning to false), but the user receives no feedback about why the deletion failed.
refutation: False positive. The reviewer concluded delete failures are silent because the onDelete call only passes onSuccess (page.tsx:733) and useDeleteLessonMutation has no per-mutation onError. But React Query applies a GLOBAL mutation error handler defined in the QueryClient defaultOptions.mutations.onError (frontend/app/providers.tsx:30-35), which fires for every mutation that does not opt out via meta.skipErrorToast. On a delete failure it calls emitToast(getErrorMessage(err), "error"), rendered by the GlobalToastHost mounted in the same providers tree (providers.tsx:52). useDeleteLessonMutation (queries.ts:1822-1831) sets no meta.skipErrorToast, so it inherits this default. A per-call onSuccess does NOT suppress the default onError — they coexist in React Query. The only intentional silent case is HTTP 401, which redirects to /login. Therefore the user DOES receive an error toast when a lesson delete fails, and the Inspector re-enabling the Delete button (deleting returns to false) is correct retry-able behavior, not silent failure. The QA review missed the centralized error-toast wiring in providers.tsx.

frontend/lib/queries.ts:1822-1831 — useDeleteLessonMutation sets no meta.skipErrorToast, inherits global onError
frontend/app/providers.tsx:25-36 — defaultOptions.mutations.onError emits error toast for all non-401 mutations not opting out

expected_behaviour: A minimap is built as a Phaser Container in the top-left corner (scrollFactor 0). It shows each star as a coloured dot proportional to its world position, sized relative to the minimap. The current ship position is shown as a distinct marker. During map mode, the current camera view rectangle is drawn on the minimap. The minimap updates every frame. If a waypoint is set, the waypoint target appears on the minimap.
original_claim: The expected behavior specifies the minimap in the top-left corner but the implementation places it in the bottom-right corner. The comment at line 2057 explicitly reads 'Fixed bottom-right minimap' and positionMinimap() at lines 2121-2127 computes position as (scale.width - mapWidth - margin, scale.height - mapHeight - margin). Functionally the minimap is correct (star dots, ship marker, view rect, waypoint, per-frame updates) but the documented location mismatches reality, which may cause confusion if a user follows any help text pointing them to the top-left.
refutation: Not a real defect. The factual code claim is accurate: the minimap is positioned bottom-right (comment at galaxy-scene.ts:2057 "Fixed bottom-right minimap"; positionMinimap() at 2120-2127 sets x = scale.width - miniW - margin, y = scale.height - miniH - margin), while the audit record's expected_behaviour for FA-0261 says "top-left corner." But the "top-left" expectation is the QA tool's own auto-generated description (a misread of the code), not an authoritative product spec or any user-facing text. There is NO help text, tooltip, tutorial, or documentation anywhere in the frontend pointing users to a top-left minimap: grepping the entire frontend (excluding node_modules) for "top-left"/"top left"/"minimap"/"bottom-right" returns ONLY galaxy-scene.ts itself. The hypothesized harm ("may cause confusion if a user follows any help text pointing them to the top-left") therefore cannot occur because no such text exists. The minimap is self-evident, display-only, and functionally correct (star dots, ship marker, view rect, waypoint, per-frame updates per lines 2089-2095 and 2129-2157), and the code comment and implementation agree with each other. A real user simply sees the minimap in the corner where it is and is not misdirected. This is an internal spec-vs-implementation discrepancy in the audit record with zero user impact.

frontend/lib/constellation-game/galaxy-scene.ts:2120-2127 (positionMinimap computes scale.width/height - size - margin = bottom-right)
frontend/lib/constellation-game/galaxy-scene.ts:2089-2095,2129-2157 (functionally correct dots/marker/view-rect/waypoint)

expected_behaviour: A 'Delete' button in rose text is always visible in the action bar regardless of edit mode. Clicking shows window.confirm('Delete this workout?'). On confirm: calls DELETE /api/v1/fuel/workouts/{id}/, clears the autosave draft, and closes the drawer. No pending/loading state on the button (the mutation is fire-and-forget from the drawer's perspective since it closes immediately).
original_claim: The expected behaviour states the Delete button has 'rose text' in the action bar, but the code renders it as text-ink-muted (gray) by default; only on hover does it become rose. The button is visually indistinguishable from other neutral actions until hovered, reducing the destructive affordance. This is a design/spec discrepancy but low user-impact since the confirm dialog still guards the action.
refutation: Not a real defect. The code at workout-detail.tsx:826 does render the Delete button as `text-ink-muted hover:text-rose-text` (gray by default, rose on hover), exactly as cited — but this is intended, design-system-consistent behaviour, not a violation of any actual spec. The "rose text always visible" expectation comes solely from the audit record's self-authored expected_behaviour, not from any documented design rule: DESIGN.md has no destructive-action/rose token requirement, and git blame shows the button has rendered this exact way since it was first written (2026-04-22) — it was never rose-by-default. The muted-until-hover pattern is the established convention across the codebase for secondary/inline delete affordances: every comparable delete control in the fuel area (sleep.tsx:97, body-weight.tsx:121, resting-heart-rate.tsx:96, and the inline delete buttons within workout-detail.tsx itself at 993/1018/1356/1388) uses `text-ink-faint/muted hover:text-rose-text`. Persistent always-rose styling is reserved for error states, status badges, and primary destructive-confirm buttons (dynamic-list.tsx:32, pending-reminders-section.tsx:201). The workout-detail Delete sits among neutral siblings (Edit workout, Duplicate, Save as template) and correctly follows the secondary pattern — it turns rose on hover/focus, providing the destructive affordance at the moment of interaction. The action is additionally fully guarded by window.confirm('Delete this workout?'), so there is no accidental-deletion risk. A real user experiences a reasonable, conventional destructive control, not a defect; the only "discrepancy" is against the auditor's own assumed spec.

feature: Pending goal extraction — accept as goal or task
expected_behaviour: The 'Suggestions from your journal' section is only rendered when `data.pending_extractions.length > 0`. Each `HorizonsPendingExtraction` renders a `PendingGoal` card with either an 'AI Extraction' badge (accent colours, for kind='goal') or 'Pattern Found' badge (signal colours, for kind='task'), showing the extracted quote, confidence level, and source date. Clicking 'Accept Goal' or 'Accept Task' fires `useApproveExtractionMutation` → `POST /api/v1/journal/extractions/<id>/approve/`. While pending the button text changes to 'Saving...' and both buttons are disabled. On success, local `resolved` state is set to 'approved' and the card body is replaced with 'Added to your goals!' or 'Added to your tasks!' (signal green). The ['horizons'] query is also invalidated. Clicking 'Dismiss' fires `useDismissExtractionMutation` → `POST /api/v1/journal/extractions/<id>/dismiss/`. On success, `resolved` is set to 'dismissed' and the card unmounts entirely (returns null).
original_claim: When approveExtraction or dismissExtraction returns an API error, no error state is surfaced to the user: `onError` is never passed to either mutate call, so the card silently returns to its idle state with buttons re-enabled. The user receives no feedback that their action failed and may not know to retry.
refutation: False positive -- error feedback IS surfaced, just not where the reviewer looked. The QueryClient is constructed with a global mutation default onError handler (frontend/app/providers.tsx:25-36) that fires for EVERY mutation unless it opts out via meta.skipErrorToast. On failure it calls emitToast(getErrorMessage(err), "error"), rendered by GlobalToastHost mounted in the providers tree. The only skipped case is HTTP 401, which is intentionally silent because apiFetch redirects to /login. Neither useApproveExtractionMutation nor useDismissExtractionMutation (frontend/lib/queries.ts:267-285) sets meta.skipErrorToast, so a failed approve/dismiss DOES produce a visible error toast. The card correctly returns to its idle state with buttons re-enabled so the user can retry -- that is desired behavior, not a silent failure. The claim (and the feature record's own inventory note 'error states from the mutations are not shown to the user') is a misread that assumed error handling only lives in the per-mutation onSuccess/onError callbacks and never examined the QueryClient default-options layer.
refutation_evidence: frontend/app/providers.tsx:13-43 (QueryClient defaultOptions.mutations.onError -> emitToast for all non-401 errors unless meta.skipErrorToast)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:267-285 (approve/dismiss mutations do NOT set meta.skipErrorToast, so covered by the global toast)
frontend/components/toast.tsx:22,111 (emitToast emitter + GlobalToastHost renderer)
frontend/components/pending-goal.tsx:65-92 (card re-enables buttons on idle, allowing retry after a failure)

expected_behaviour: TelegramCard shows a StatusPill('pending') and a 'Connect' button when status.linked is false. Clicking calls generateTelegramLink() → POST /api/v1/tenants/telegram/generate-link/. While pending the button label changes to 'Generating...' and is disabled. On success the response (qr_code data-URL and deep_link) is stored in local state; a QR image and an 'Open in Telegram' link (target=_blank) are shown. The useTelegramStatusQuery polls at 3 s (pairingActive=true) while the QR is visible and at 15 s otherwise; it stops polling once linked=true. Errors from the mutation are silently suppressed (caught, no UI).
original_claim: When POST /api/v1/tenants/telegram/generate-link/ fails (network error, server 5xx, etc.), the catch block at line 57 silently suppresses the error with no UI feedback. The user sees the unchanged 'Connect' button without any indication that link generation failed.
refutation: False positive. The claim asserts the catch block at lines 57-58 silently suppresses the error with no UI feedback, but it overlooks the GLOBAL mutation error handler. The app's QueryClient (frontend/app/providers.tsx:25-36) sets defaultOptions.mutations.onError to emit a toast for every failed mutation: `emitToast(getErrorMessage(err), "error")`, skipping only 401 (handled by apiFetch redirect) or mutations that opt out via meta.skipErrorToast. useGenerateTelegramLinkMutation (frontend/lib/queries.ts:491-495) is a bare useMutation with NO skipErrorToast meta and NO custom onError, so it falls through to the global handler. For the cited failure modes (network error, server 5xx) the status is not 401, so an error toast IS shown via the GlobalToastHost mounted in providers.tsx:52. The component's empty catch with the comment `// handled by mutation` (line 58) is therefore accurate — it deliberately defers feedback to the global mutation handler, and the button correctly reverts from "Generating..." to "Connect" because generateLink.isPending returns to false. The user does get UI feedback (a toast). No defect.

expected_behaviour: LineCard shows a 'Connect LINE' button (LINE-green border) when status.linked is false. Clicking calls generateLineLink() → POST /api/v1/tenants/line/generate-link/. While pending the button is disabled with label 'Generating...'. On success a QR image, a step-by-step instruction box, an 'Open in LINE' link, and a '15 minutes' expiry note are shown. The useLineStatusQuery polls at 3 s while QR is visible, 15 s otherwise, stops once linked=true. Errors are silently suppressed.
original_claim: When POST /api/v1/tenants/line/generate-link/ fails, the catch block at line 145 suppresses the error silently. The user sees no indication that link generation failed and the Connect LINE button remains in its original state with no feedback.
refutation: False positive. The reviewer read the empty `catch {}` at page.tsx:145-147 in isolation and missed the global React Query error handler. The app's QueryClient defines a `MutationCache`-level `mutations.onError` in app/providers.tsx:30-35 that calls `emitToast(getErrorMessage(err), "error")` for EVERY failed mutation unless the mutation opts out via `meta.skipErrorToast`. `useGenerateLineLinkMutation` (lib/queries.ts:537-541) sets no `meta`, so a failed POST /tenants/line/generate-link/ DOES surface a visible error toast to the user. The empty `catch {}` in `handleConnect` only swallows the awaited promise rejection to prevent an unhandled rejection — the actual user-facing feedback is the global toast, which is what the "handled by mutation" comment refers to. The button correctly returns from "Generating..." to "Connect LINE" (generateLink.isPending → false) on failure, which is the desired end state, and the user is informed via toast. emitToast/getErrorMessage/GlobalToastHost are all wired and mounted (providers.tsx:7,9,52). The claim that "the user sees no indication that link generation failed" is contradicted by the code.

expected_behaviour: Clicking 'Revoke' on a TokenRow opens RevokeConfirmModal with the token name shown ('X will stop working immediately. Any app using it will need a new token.'). A 'Revoke' button calls revokePAT(pat.id) → DELETE /api/v1/auth/tokens/<id>/revoke/ (via the revokePAT function). An optimistic update removes the token from the ['pats'] cache immediately. The button shows 'Revoking...' and is disabled while pending. On success, the modal closes and the cache is invalidated. On error, the previous state is restored and a rose error message is shown inside the modal. Cancel closes without action. Escape key also closes.
original_claim: The feature inventory describes the revoke endpoint as DELETE /api/v1/auth/tokens/<id>/revoke/ (with a /revoke/ suffix). The actual backend URL is tokens/<uuid:token_id>/ (no /revoke/ suffix) and the frontend calls DELETE /api/v1/auth/tokens/${id}/ to match. The code is internally consistent and works correctly, but if any external caller (e.g., documentation, a third-party integration) relies on the /revoke/ path described in the inventory, it will get a 404. No user-visible breakage in the current UI flow.
refutation: False positive. The "/revoke/" suffix exists ONLY in the feature-audit inventory's own prose (the FA-0374 JSON record), not in any product code. The actual code is fully consistent: the backend registers path("tokens/<uuid:token_id>/", PATRevokeView.as_view()) whose delete() method revokes the token (auth_urls.py:34, pat_views.py:99-116), and the frontend revokePAT(id) calls DELETE /api/v1/auth/tokens/${id}/ (api.ts:1522-1524), wired through useRevokePATMutation (queries.ts:1713). A grep for any "revoke/" path across pat_views.py, frontend/lib, and the connected-apps page returns no matches. The PAT endpoints form a private subscriber-console / YardTalk-PAT API; there is no published external REST contract or third-party integration that consumes a "/revoke/" path, so the inventory's hypothetical "external caller gets a 404" cannot occur. The discrepancy is an error in the audit document describing the feature, not a defect in the running product — no user or operator experiences any breakage. Any correction belongs to the inventory text, not the codebase.

feature: Create new goal or project document
expected_behaviour: Addable kinds (goal, project) show a + button next to the section header. Clicking it reveals an inline text input (auto-focused) and an Add button. The user types a name; Enter or clicking Add calls createMutation.mutate({kind, slug: slugify(name), title: name, markdown: ''}), which POSTs to POST /api/v1/journal/documents/. While pending, the Add button shows '…' and is disabled. On success, the sidebar tree is invalidated, the form is cleared, and onNavigate is called with the new document's kind and slug. Pressing Escape cancels. Empty or whitespace-only names are rejected client-side (button disabled, Enter guard). The slug is auto-computed by lowercasing, collapsing spaces to hyphens, and stripping non-alphanumeric characters.
original_claim: When createMutation fails (e.g., duplicate slug violates the unique_together constraint on tenant+kind+slug), the sidebar shows no error message — createMutation.isError is never rendered. The inline form stays open until a blur fires the 150ms cancel timer, silently discarding the failure. Users have no indication that their document was not created.
refutation: False positive — the defect's core premise is factually wrong. It claims a duplicate slug "violates the unique_together constraint on tenant+kind+slug" and causes createMutation to fail silently. But the server-side create handler (apps/journal/document_views.py:180-197, DocumentListCreateView.post) uses Document.objects.get_or_create(tenant, kind, slug, ...), which queries before inserting. A colliding slug therefore returns HTTP 200 with the EXISTING document — it never reaches the INSERT, so the unique_together constraint at models.py:199 is never triggered and no IntegrityError is raised. The DocumentCreateSerializer (document_serializers.py:31-35) is a plain Serializer with no UniqueTogetherValidator or validate(), so it also never 400s on duplicates. As a result createMutation resolves successfully: onSuccess (sidebar.tsx:178-182) closes the inline form and navigates the user to the (existing) document via onNavigate — a reasonable, non-error outcome. The createMutation.isError path the finding worries about is simply not reached for the described duplicate-slug scenario. While it is true the sidebar has no onError/isError handling for genuinely unexpected failures (network drop, 401, 500), that generic concern is not the data-integrity scenario claimed, and the specific user-facing defect described does not occur.
refutation_evidence: apps/journal/document_views.py:180-197 (post uses get_or_create → 200/201, no IntegrityError on duplicate)
apps/journal/document_serializers.py:31-35 (plain Serializer, no UniqueTogetherValidator/validate)
apps/journal/models.py:199 (unique_together exists but is never hit due to get_or_create)
frontend/components/journal/sidebar.tsx:171-185 (confirmAdd → onSuccess closes form and navigates; duplicate resolves as success)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:928-937 (useCreateDocumentMutation has no onError, but failure path described does not occur)

feature: Current status card — obligations, tasks, goals
expected_behaviour: CurrentStatusCard is rendered above the document content only when kind === 'daily' and not editing. It calls useJournalStatusQuery (GET /api/v1/journal/status/, staleTime 60s). While loading or if data is undefined, it renders null. If data has no obligations, no open_tasks, and no active_goals, it renders null (completely hidden). Otherwise it shows a card with 'Current status · as of now'. Obligations render nickname, minimum_payment (formatted as $X.XX), due_date (short format), and a status pill (green for paid, amber for partial, rose for unpaid/overdue with 'Overdue' label override). Open tasks show a checkbox button; clicking it calls completeTaskMutation (POST /api/v1/journal/tasks/{id}/complete/) which invalidates journal-status on success. Tasks may show a due_date. Active goals show title and optional target_date.
original_claim: A single shared completeTaskMutation instance is used for all open tasks in CurrentStatusCard. When any one task is being completed, completeTaskMutation.isPending is true and all task checkboxes are disabled simultaneously (line 86). A user with multiple open tasks cannot queue a second completion until the first network round-trip finishes, and there is no per-row loading indicator — just the blanket disabled state. This is visible but not broken; however, it diverges from the expected behaviour which implies per-item interactivity.
refutation: The code matches the claim exactly — a single shared completeTaskMutation (current-status-card.tsx:45) drives every open-task checkbox, and each button is disabled via completeTaskMutation.isPending (line 86), so during any one completion all checkboxes are momentarily disabled with no per-row spinner. But this is NOT a real defect: (1) The feature's expected_behaviour only requires that clicking a checkbox calls completeTaskMutation and invalidates journal-status on success — it never mandates per-item interactivity or per-row loading indicators. The audit's own inventory_notes explicitly document the blanket-disabled/no-spinner state as known existing design ("disabled while isPending but shows no loading indicator beyond the disabled state"), i.e. intended, not a divergence. (2) The disabled window is a single sub-second network round-trip; on success the mutation invalidates the journal-status query (queries.ts:251-253) and the completed task drops off the refetched list, so state clears on re-render. (3) The blanket disable is a benign guard against double-submits and racing the invalidation refetch. There is no correctness, data-integrity, accessibility, or functional break — a user can complete every task, just not fire two in the same brief in-flight window. At most an optional polish nicety, below the bar for a reportable UX defect.

feature: Scroll-triggered header border on legal pages
expected_behaviour: AppShell attaches a passive `scroll` event listener to the `#main-content` element. When `scrollTop > 8px`, the `headerBorder` state is set to `true`, which adds a visible bottom border to the sticky header. On legal pages the header is rendered (AppShell wraps all pages), so this behaviour applies. Scrolling back to the top removes the border. No API call; no legal-page-specific code — inherited from AppShell.
original_claim: The scroll-triggered header border does not function on legal pages. The `isPublicPage` branch renders a plain (non-sticky) header with no `headerBorder` state binding, so even if the listener fired it would have no visible effect. Additionally, the `#main-content` element in the public-page branch has no height constraint or `overflow-y-auto`, so page scrolling happens at the window/document level — the `scroll` event listener attached to that element never receives events and `main.scrollTop` stays 0 permanently. The expected behaviour (visible bottom border when scrolled) is absent for all anonymous users on legal pages.
refutation: False positive. The two technical observations are correct (the public/legal layout header is non-sticky with a static border and no headerBorder binding; the public #main-content is not a scroll container so its scroll listener never fires for this layout), but the conclusion that this is a defect is a misread of intended design. Legal pages route through the isPublicPage branch (lines 318-332), which is a deliberately DIFFERENT layout from the authenticated sticky-header layout (lines 343-349). On legal pages the header is intentionally non-sticky: it scrolls up and away with the page content, and it carries a permanent always-on separator (border-b border-white/[0.04], line 323). The scroll-reactive headerBorder border belongs only to the authenticated layout's sticky header, where it matters because that header stays pinned and needs a separator once content slides under it. On a non-sticky header a scroll-triggered border would be pointless (you cannot see the header once scrolled past). The user story's actual goal — a visible header separator and a sense of having scrolled — is fully satisfied on legal pages: the static border is always visible, and the header physically scrolling out of view is itself the scroll cue. The 'expected behaviour' in the feature record was fabricated by projecting the authenticated layout's mechanism onto the public layout. No anonymous user experiences a missing or broken separator; nothing is broken.

feature: Integrations — Telegram connect via QR code / deep link
expected_behaviour: The TelegramCard renders a pending StatusPill and a "Connect" button when `status.linked` is false. Clicking Connect calls `POST /api/v1/tenants/telegram/generate-link/`, which returns `{ qr_code, deep_link }`. A QR code image and an "Open in Telegram" button then appear. The component polls `GET /api/v1/tenants/telegram/status/` every 3 s while the link is visible (15 s otherwise). When `status.linked` becomes true, the card switches to the connected state showing `@username`. The pending link is not automatically expired client-side.
original_claim: After `POST /api/v1/tenants/telegram/generate-link/` returns a link with an `expires_at` timestamp, the `TelegramCard` stores the result in `linkData` state but never clears it or shows a countdown when the server-side token expires. A user who leaves the pairing screen open past the expiry window will still see the QR code and "Open in Telegram" button; scanning or tapping at that point silently fails in Telegram with no feedback in the UI. No countdown or expiry indicator is rendered.
refutation:
Not a defect — the absence of a client-side countdown / auto-clear / on-expiry feedback is documented intended behavior. The FA-0453 feature record's own expected_behaviour states verbatim: "The pending link is not automatically expired client-side."

Code confirms the mechanics: TelegramCard (integrations/page.tsx:43-130) stores linkData (which carries expires_at, defined in api.ts:449-453) but never reads expires_at, renders no countdown, and never auto-clears the QR/deep-link. However, the "silently fails with no feedback" framing is overstated and the real flow is self-correcting: (1) pairing is an immediate action — the user generates the link and scans/taps it right then, not 15 minutes later; (2) the card polls GET .../telegram/status/ every 3s while the link is visible (page.tsx:47) and flips to the connected "@username" state the moment pairing succeeds; (3) the card never falsely claims success — it stays in the "pending" state until status.linked becomes true, so an expired-link attempt simply leaves the user where they started, and Telegram itself surfaces the failed pairing on its side; (4) the expiry-then-retry scenario is an edge case outside the normal flow.

The only genuine (and minor/cosmetic) observation is an inconsistency, NOT the claimed defect: the LINE card shows a static "Link expires in 15 minutes" hint (page.tsx:219) while the Telegram card shows no expiry hint. That is a small copy-parity gap, not a silent-failure logic bug, and the specifically claimed "missing countdown / auto-clear on expiry" behavior is exactly what the spec intends.

expected_behaviour: When `integration.status === 'active'` or the integration record exists, a "Disconnect" button appears. Clicking calls `useDisconnectIntegrationMutation` with `integration.id`. Button shows "Disconnecting..." while pending. No confirmation dialog. On success the integrations query is invalidated and the card reverts to pending state.
original_claim: When both an active integration and a revoked integration exist for the same provider, the single shared `disconnect` mutation means clicking Disconnect for one provider disables the Disconnect button for all providers while the request is in flight. Additionally, no confirmation dialog is shown before disconnecting, which matches the spec but could cause accidental disconnection.
refutation:
Not a defect. The claim has two parts, both of which fail to confirm:

1) "Two Disconnect buttons for the same provider (one active + one revoked) all get disabled together" — this is a misread of the rendering. The card grid iterates the STATIC `providers` array (only `google` and `reddit`, page.tsx:30-41) and resolves each provider to a SINGLE integration via `data?.find((item) => item.provider === provider.key)` (page.tsx:585). At most one card and one Disconnect button is rendered per provider; two buttons for the same provider can never coexist, even if multiple integration records existed server-side. The shared `disconnect.isPending` (page.tsx:638) is shared across the two DISTINCT provider cards (google vs reddit), not across "active vs revoked of the same provider." So clicking Disconnect on Google momentarily disables the Disconnect button on Reddit. This is a minor cosmetic edge: the optimistic `onMutate` (queries.ts:445-451) immediately removes the disconnected row so its card flips out of the `isConnected` branch right away, the in-flight window is a single brief request, and disconnecting two different providers in the same instant is a rare action with no data-integrity or broken-state consequence.

2) "No confirmation dialog could cause accidental disconnection" — the feature spec (expected_behaviour in fe-settings__2.json FA-0461) explicitly states "No confirmation dialog." This is documented intended behaviour, not a defect.

Net: the described user-facing failure does not exist as framed, and the no-confirm behaviour matches spec.

feature: Scheduled Tasks — timezone selector (in cron forms)
expected_behaviour: The `<TimezoneSelector>` renders two controls: a text search input and a grouped `<select>`. It queries `Intl.supportedValuesOf('timeZone')` client-side for the full IANA list (falls back to `['UTC']`). Typing in the search filters by timezone name or UTC offset label. When the filter hides the currently-selected timezone, the component auto-selects the first visible option and fires `onChange`. Groups are derived from the IANA prefix (e.g. "America", "Europe", "Asia"). No API call.
original_claim: When the search filter hides the currently-selected timezone, the component silently auto-selects and fires `onChange` with the first visible option, updating the parent form without any user action. A user who types a search to verify their timezone selection may inadvertently change it to an unintended value before they realise what happened.
refutation:
Not a real defect. The auto-select effect (timezone-selector.tsx:121-125) fires onChange(filteredTimezones[0]) only when the search filter excludes the currently-selected timezone — and it is a deliberate consistency mechanism, documented in the inline comment, to keep the visible <select> value (which renders currentValue at lines 144/115-117) in sync with what would actually be saved. Without it, the displayed dropdown value could silently diverge from the form's stored value, which is the worse bug.

Three reasons the hypothesized harm does not materialize: (1) In all three call sites (cron-jobs/page.tsx:594, 968, 1033) onChange only mutates LOCAL form state (createForm.tz / editForm.tz). Nothing is persisted to the backend — the value is only saved on an explicit form submit (handleCreate / handleUpdate). (2) The change is fully visible: the <select> immediately re-renders showing the newly auto-selected timezone, so the user reviews it in the dropdown before submitting. (3) There is no distinct "verify my timezone" mode the QA scenario assumes — the search box exists solely to narrow the list for picking a timezone while building/editing a cron schedule; a user typing into it is in the act of selecting, not passively inspecting. The combination of visible reflection + no auto-save + an explicit-submit gate means a user would not inadvertently persist an unintended timezone. Intended behavior, adequately guarded.

feature: Finance USER.md Envelope Section (Gravity Context Injection)
expected_behaviour: apps/finance/envelope.py:render_finance is registered as a USER.md section with key='finance', heading='## Gravity — finance state', order=50. Enabled gate: tenant.finance_active (folds in GRAVITY_ENABLED platform pause). refresh_on=(FinanceAccount, FinanceTransaction, PayoffPlan) — section is regenerated whenever any of these models change for the tenant. Renders: active account count and total debt, the active payoff plan summary (strategy/months/payoff date/budget), top-priority debt by strategy (avalanche: highest APR; else snowball: lowest balance), upcoming due dates within 7 days (modular 31-day arithmetic), and last 3 transactions from the past 14 days. Truncates at max_chars=1000 with a note directing the agent to call nbhd_finance_summary for full state.
original_claim: The upcoming-due-date filter `0 <= ((a.due_day - today.day) % 31) <= 7` produces false positives near month-end. For example on the 28th with due_day=3, the result is (3-28)%31=6 which falls in range 0-7, incorrectly reporting the 3rd of next month (~5-6 days away) as upcoming this week. The modulus 31 is not the actual number of days remaining in the month, so any due date in the first week of next month appears due within 7 days when today is in the final week. The user sees phantom urgent reminders in their AI context.
refutation:
The claimed defect — phantom/false-positive "upcoming due" reminders near month-end — does NOT occur. I verified the filter 0 <= ((a.due_day - today.day) % 31) <= 7 (apps/finance/envelope.py:77) by enumerating every (month, day, due_day) pair across a full year against the real number of days until the next actual due date: there are ZERO false positives. The formula never flags a payment that isn't genuinely due within 7 days.

The QA's own cited example actually disproves the claim: on the 28th with due_day=3, (3-28)%31 = 6, and the 3rd of next month is genuinely 6 days away — so flagging it is CORRECT, not a phantom reminder. The user would not see fake urgency.

There IS a real bug in this line, but it is the OPPOSITE of what was claimed: the %31 arithmetic produces false NEGATIVES (56 cases/year, concentrated in February and 30-day months). e.g. on Feb 22, due_day=1 (March 1) is genuinely 7 days away, but (1-22)%31 = 10 > 7, so it is NOT flagged — the user MISSES a real upcoming reminder near short-month boundaries; it does not invent extra ones. Because the claimed defect (phantom urgent reminders / false positives / user-visible fake urgency) is factually wrong, this verdict is a false positive. The genuine inverse issue is low-severity (a missed nudge in non-authoritative AI context that also surfaces nbhd_finance_summary for full state) and is not the defect under review.

apps/finance/models.py:66 (due_day IntegerField 1-31)
Full-year enumeration of all (date,due_day) pairs: 0 false positives, 56 false negatives in Feb/30-day month boundaries — claim of phantom reminders is inverted

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/fuel/workouts/{workout_id}/edit-lock/ acquires a lock for FUEL_EDIT_LOCK_TTL_SECONDS (default 60s), sets edit_lock_owner='user' and edit_lock_until. Returns `{workout_id, edit_lock_until, edit_lock_owner, ttl_seconds, version}`. The browser heartbeats this at half the TTL. DELETE /api/v1/fuel/workouts/{workout_id}/edit-lock/ releases the lock (idempotent — returns 204 even if already released). Runtime PATCH/DELETE/skip/complete paths check `_edit_locked_response`: if lock is in the future they return 409 with `{error: edit_locked, lock_owner, retry_after_s, edit_lock_until, workout_id}` plus a Retry-After header. JWT auth required for both lock endpoints.
original_claim: The `Workout.version` field returned by the edit-lock POST response (and described in the model help_text as 'Bumped on every save') is never actually incremented by consumer PATCH, complete, or skip paths — there is no pre_save signal and no manual bump in those handlers. Only the HealthKit ingest path manually increments it. A frontend relying on version for optimistic-concurrency (If-Match) will always see a stale counter (default 0 for most rows), making the locking guarantee illusory for non-HealthKit workouts.
refutation: The factual code observation is accurate but the claimed user impact is fabricated, so this is a false positive. Confirmed facts: Workout.version is incremented only in the HealthKit ingest path (healthkit.py:314, 434); there is no pre_save signal, and the consumer PATCH (views.py:335-364), complete (views.py:771-794), skip (views.py:751-762), and all runtime handlers (runtime_views.py patch/complete/skip) call .save() without bumping it. The post_save signals (signals.py:109-126) bump tenant.fuel_version, a SEPARATE tenant-level polling counter, NOT Workout.version. The model help_text (models.py:254-258) overstates that version is 'Bumped on every save' and 'Used by clients for optimistic-concurrency If-Match checks.' However, the claimed defect (a frontend relying on version for If-Match always seeing a stale counter, making the locking guarantee illusory) describes a consumer that does not exist: there is NO If-Match / HTTP_IF_MATCH handler anywhere in the backend, and no frontend or iOS code reads workout.version or sends If-Match. The real, functioning concurrency mechanism is edit_lock_until, which makes runtime PATCH/complete/skip return 409 during the lock window (runtime_views.py:377, 414), entirely independent of version. version is merely exposed read-only in the serializer (serializers.py:94/105) and echoed in the edit-lock POST response (views.py:418); it is effectively dead/unused metadata. No user or operator experiences any failure since there is no version-based locking guarantee in production to be illusory. At most this is a stale/over-promising docstring on an unused field, not a logic defect with user impact.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/insights/compare/?pillar=gravity&period_a=<uuid>&period_b=<uuid> requires JWT authentication. Both period_a and period_b UUIDs are mandatory; missing either returns 400 {error: missing_param}. Both snapshots must belong to the authenticated tenant and the requested pillar; otherwise 404. Returns both serialized snapshots plus a totals_delta dict with signed string deltas for debt, savings, and minimum_payments (period_b minus period_a). For gravity the _gravity_totals_delta function computes Decimal deltas with explicit sign prefix.
original_claim: When period_a and period_b are the same UUID, the DB query deduplicates them via id__in and returns one row. Both keys map to the same snapshot object, so the 404 guard passes and the diff is computed against itself — producing all-zero deltas with no error or warning. The assistant receives a misleading zero-change result instead of a 400 rejecting the no-op comparison.
refutation: The described mechanics are accurate: with period_a == period_b, id__in=[a_id, b_id] queries one row, the dict comprehension {str(s.id): s for s in ...} yields a single entry, both a_id/b_id resolve to the same key so the 404 guard passes, and diff = b - a computes all-zero deltas (views.py:200-206, mirrored at runtime_views.py:193-199). However this is NOT a defect. (1) The expected_behaviour spec only mandates both UUIDs be present (400 if missing) and belong to the tenant+pillar (404 otherwise); it does NOT require rejecting identical IDs. (2) The result is mathematically truthful, not misleading: the change between a snapshot and itself genuinely is zero across debt/savings/minimum_payments. (3) The response returns both serialized snapshots, which are byte-for-byte identical, self-evidently signaling the no-op to any consumer (assistant or frontend). (4) It is a read-only diff endpoint with no data-integrity, security, or error-state impact; nothing breaks, loops, or corrupts. Rejecting self-comparison with a 400 would be a minor input-validation nicety, not a fix for a real harm. No real user or operator experiences this as a bug.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/insights/voice-prefs/set/ requires JWT authentication. Body must include pillar (in ALLOWED_PILLARS) and register_offset (-1, 0, or +1). Optional fields: tone (gentle|direct), volume (silent|weekly|live), topic (slug string). If topic is provided and not found in TopicRegistry, resolve_topic auto-creates a proposed entry. Performs update_or_create keyed on (tenant, pillar, topic) so the operation is idempotent. Returns the serialized voice pref. Invalid register_offset or enum values return 400.
original_claim: _validate_voice_pref_body returns 404 when the pillar field is absent or empty. An empty string '' is not in ALLOWED_PILLARS, so a completely missing pillar field returns 404 {error: pillar_not_available} rather than a 400 indicating a missing required field. Clients that omit pillar receive a confusing 404 rather than a validation error.
refutation: Not a real defect. _validate_voice_pref_body normalizes pillar via (data.get("pillar") or "").strip().lower() and rejects anything not in ALLOWED_PILLARS with a 404 pillar_not_available, so a missing/empty pillar yields the same 404. That is consistent with the documented contract — FA-0600 expected_behaviour states "Body must include pillar (in ALLOWED_PILLARS)" — and it is the deliberate, repeated convention across ~9 call sites in apps/insights/views.py and runtime_views.py for the single-pillar gate (ALLOWED_PILLARS is currently {GRAVITY}). The 404 response is self-describing: it returns "pillar": "" and "allowed": [...], so the caller can immediately see they sent an empty/missing pillar and what is permitted — it is not "confusing." Crucially, the only real consumer is the assistant tool nbhd_insights_voice_pref_set(pillar, topic, register_offset) (documented in apps/insights/envelope.py), which always supplies pillar; there is no frontend/TS or other client that omits it, so the "client forgets pillar -> confusing 404" scenario does not occur in practice. The complaint is pure HTTP-status purism (404 vs 400) with no user/operator-facing impact.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/insights/runtime/<tenant_uuid>/compare/?pillar=gravity&period_a=<uuid>&period_b=<uuid> authenticates via internal key headers. Requires both period_a and period_b query params (400 if either is missing). Both snapshots must belong to the tenant and the specified pillar; otherwise 404. Returns both serialized snapshots plus totals_delta (signed string deltas for debt/savings/minimum_payments for gravity).
original_claim: Same as FA-0593: when period_a and period_b are the same UUID, the id__in query deduplicates to one row, both guard checks pass, and the assistant receives all-zero deltas with no error. The runtime path is called by OpenClaw during conversations and would silently mislead the assistant into reporting no change between two periods.
refutation: Not a real defect. When period_a == period_b, the id__in=[a_id, b_id] filter does dedup to a single PillarSnapshot row, and both 'a_id not in snaps' / 'b_id not in snaps' guards pass because both query params are the same string and that key exists in the dict (runtime_views.py:193-197). The endpoint then computes _gravity_totals_delta(a.payload, a.payload) = debt-debt, savings-savings, minimum_payments-minimum_payments = all zeros (views.py:156-170). But this output is ARITHMETICALLY CORRECT, not misleading: comparing a snapshot to itself genuinely has zero change. The response also serializes BOTH period_a and period_b as the (identical) snapshot, so the consumer receives complete, truthful information — two identical snapshots with zero deltas. The claim of 'silently mislead into reporting no change' is unsupported: there genuinely is no change between a period and itself. No data corruption, no fabricated values, no user-facing harm. This is the OpenClaw runtime endpoint; passing the same UUID twice would be a degenerate caller choice, and the honest zero-delta answer is the correct response. Rejecting X-vs-X would be a gratuitous validation, not a correctness fix; many compare APIs legitimately allow self-comparison.
