feature: Insight Marker Extraction from Outbound Replies
expected_behaviour: extract_and_record_insights(text, tenant, pillar) is called at every outbound message path (Telegram poller, Telegram webhook drain, LINE webhook). It scans text with a regex for [[insight:slug]]...[/insight]] markers (case-sensitive, DOTALL). For each match: slug is passed to resolve_topic; statement is truncated to 1000 chars; an AssistantInsight row is created with status=open. Marker tokens are stripped from the text (only the statement stays in the user-facing reply). Per-marker failures are logged and do not block the reply from going out. Returns cleaned text.
original_claim: All outbound message call sites (poller, Telegram drain, LINE webhook, iOS drain) invoke extract_and_record_insights without a pillar= override, so the function defaults to pillar='gravity'. A fuel-related marker emitted by the assistant (e.g. [[insight:exercise]]...) will be resolved against the gravity topic registry, creating a spurious proposed gravity topic and recording the insight under the wrong pillar. The reply still goes out cleanly and no crash occurs, but accumulated fuel insights are miscategorised until call sites pass the correct pillar when Fuel marker rules ship.
refutation: False positive. The factual half of the claim is correct: all four outbound call sites call extract_and_record_insights(text, tenant=tenant) with no pillar= override, so it defaults to pillar='gravity' (markers.py:53). But the impact half is unfounded. The marker rules actually shipped to the assistant (templates/openclaw/rules/reply-markers.md:41-77) scope [[insight:...]] markers exclusively to Gravity: line 43 says "When a Gravity reply raises a pattern observation", and line 49 says "topic_slug should be a canonical Gravity topic — dining, debt, savings, subscriptions, discretionary, fixed_expenses, income, large_purchases". The assistant is never instructed to emit a Fuel marker such as [[insight:exercise]], so the claimed triggering input does not occur in practice. The function's own docstring (markers.py:57-60) documents that pillar='gravity' is the deliberate default "because Phase 2 / Phase 3 only target that pillar today" and that call sites "will need a pillar= override at the call site once Fuel / Core extend the rules" — i.e. the override is explicitly gated on a not-yet-shipped feature. The FA-0616 inventory note (line 96) restates this as known intended behaviour. So no real user/operator gets a miscategorised insight today: the only markers the assistant produces are Gravity markers, which the gravity default categorises correctly. This is intended, documented behaviour with a forward-looking TODO, not a present defect.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/integrations/authorize/<provider>/ — IsAuthenticated required. For non-Composio providers (google, sautai): validates that client_id and client_secret are configured in settings; builds a signed+HMAC Django state token (user_id, provider, nonce) stored in cache with 600s TTL and also in a process-local fallback dict; returns JSON {url: <authorization_url>} with full OAuth params including offline access_type and prompt=consent. For Composio providers: calls initiate_composio_connection, caches connection_request_id keyed by state, returns {url: <composio_redirect_url>}. Returns 400 for unknown providers or missing credentials.
original_claim: In _consume_state_nonce, when cache.get succeeds but cache.delete throws, the function still returns True (nonce accepted) while leaving the cache key intact. A second callback request with the same signed state would again find the cache key present and return True, enabling OAuth state nonce replay. The fallback dict is consumed correctly but the cache entry survives the delete failure.
refutation: The code path is literally as described: in _consume_state_nonce (apps/integrations/views.py:88-94), on a cache hit, if cache.delete throws, the key is left intact, the fallback-consume return value at line 93 is discarded, and line 94 returns True unconditionally, so a replay could be accepted IF delete persistently throws. But a real operator/user cannot trigger this. (1) Production uses django-redis with IGNORE_EXCEPTIONS=True (config/settings/base.py:213-232): cache.get/cache.delete swallow Redis errors and return None instead of raising. The realistic failure mode (Redis unavailable) therefore makes cache_hit=False (never enters the cache-hit branch) rather than producing 'get succeeds but delete throws.' The try/except around delete guards a path the production backend will not exercise. (2) The defect would require the near-impossible transient where Redis serves a read but raises only on the delete, with the same signed state replayed inside the 600s TTL. (3) Defense-in-depth: the state is an HMAC-signed token loaded via signing.loads(max_age=600) (lines 119, 109), so even absent the nonce, replay is bounded to 10 minutes and the attacker must already hold a valid signed state that is only ever delivered to the legitimate user's browser. Net real-world exposure is negligible; this is a defensive-branch code imperfection, not a defect users/operators experience.
refutation_evidence: apps/integrations/views.py:79-102 (_consume_state_nonce cache-hit branch returns True even when cache.delete raises; fallback return value ignored)
apps/integrations/views.py:105-125 (_build_oauth_state records fallback then cache.set; _load_oauth_state uses signing.loads max_age=600)
apps/integrations/views.py:34-36 (OAUTH_STATE_MAX_AGE_SECONDS=600, process-local fallback dict)
config/settings/base.py:213-237 (production Redis cache OPTIONS IGNORE_EXCEPTIONS=True so get/delete swallow errors and do not raise)

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/integrations/runtime/<tenant_id>/reddit/complete/ — internal auth. Body: {connection_request_id (required)}. Calls complete_composio_connection which polls Composio for up to 30 seconds for status=ACTIVE, then upserts Integration row. Returns {connected: true, provider_email} HTTP 200. 400 on missing connection_request_id. 409/503 on specific integration errors. 500 on unexpected error.
original_claim: When the Composio 30-second poll times out or returns a non-ACTIVE status, complete_composio_connection raises IntegrationAccessError (the base class, line 115) or a Composio SDK timeout exception. RedditCompleteView only catches IntegrationProviderConfigError and IntegrationInactiveError explicitly; all other failures fall to the broad except Exception handler and return HTTP 500. The most common real-world failure — user takes longer than 30 seconds to authorize on Reddit — returns a generic 500 instead of a client-actionable 409, breaking the agent's ability to retry or prompt the user to try again.
refutation:
The code mechanic is accurately described, but the asserted UX defect does not manifest for any real user/operator, so it is a false positive as framed (medium/ux).

Code-level facts confirmed: complete_composio_connection raises the bare base IntegrationAccessError on non-ACTIVE status (services.py:115); a 30s wait_for_connection timeout raises a Composio SDK exception. RedditCompleteView (runtime_views.py:3370) catches only (IntegrationProviderConfigError, IntegrationInactiveError), which per services.py:241-266 are SUBCLASSES of IntegrationAccessError, not superclasses. So the bare base error and the SDK timeout fall through to except Exception (3372-3377) -> HTTP 500. _integration_error_response would map IntegrationInactiveError to 409 (lines 305-309) but that branch is never reached for the timeout/non-active path. So a 500 is indeed returned where a 409 would be more semantically apt.

Why it is NOT a real defect: (1) The /reddit/complete/ runtime endpoint is orphaned -- nothing in the repo calls it. The agent reddit plugin exposes only connect/status/digest/disconnect/tool (no complete tool); the frontend only lists reddit as a capability chip and never calls complete. The claimed impact (breaking the agent ability to retry or prompt the user) therefore cannot occur -- there is no caller, and no consumer branches on 409 vs 500. (2) The distinction is non-actionable: both 409 and 500 would surface as a generic error message; no retry/prompt logic keys off the HTTP status. (3) The auditors own expected_behaviour explicitly permits 500 on unexpected error, and a poll timeout/non-active result is reasonably unexpected. The only residue is a low code-quality inconsistency (line 115 could raise IntegrationInactiveError), not a UX defect a user experiences.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/integrations/runtime/<tenant_id>/reddit/tool/ — internal auth. Body: {action (required), ...params}. Maps action to Composio tool slug via _REDDIT_ACTION_MAP: digest→REDDIT_GET_R_TOP, search→REDDIT_SEARCH_ACROSS_SUBREDDITS, my_activity→REDDIT_GET_REDDIT_USER_ABOUT, comments→REDDIT_RETRIEVE_POST_COMMENTS, post→REDDIT_CREATE_REDDIT_POST, reply→REDDIT_POST_REDDIT_COMMENT, edit→REDDIT_EDIT_REDDIT_COMMENT_OR_POST, delete_post→REDDIT_DELETE_REDDIT_POST, delete_comment→REDDIT_DELETE_REDDIT_COMMENT, new→REDDIT_GET_NEW, controversial→REDDIT_GET_CONTROVERSIAL. original_claim: When the Reddit integration is not connected (no Integration row or missing composio_connected_account_id), execute_reddit_tool raises ValueError('Reddit integration not found or not fully connected for this tenant.'). RedditToolView catches all ValueError as {"error": "invalid_action"} HTTP 400. The error key "invalid_action" conflates a connection-state problem with a bad action name; an AI agent reading this key may retry with corrected parameters rather than prompting the user to reconnect Reddit in Settings. The detail string is readable but the discriminator key is wrong for this error class.
refutation: The code facts are accurate but the impact assessment is a false positive. execute_reddit_tool raises ValueError for the not-connected case (services.py:603-604), and RedditToolView maps ALL ValueError to error="invalid_action" HTTP 400 (runtime_views.py:3471-3475), conflating three conditions: unknown action (services.py:593-594), not-connected (603-604), and Composio-side invalid-connection (623-624). This is a discriminator-naming imprecision with no real user/agent impact, not a behavioral defect, for three reasons. (1) Unreachable in practice: the agent-facing Reddit tools live in a plugin that config_generator.py:1886-1899 only ships to the container when the tenant has an ACTIVE Reddit integration, so with no Integration row (the QA's primary scenario) the tool is absent from that container and the agent cannot call it or receive this error. (2) The ACTIVE-but-missing-composio_connected_account_id half-state is rare/transient because status ACTIVE and composio_connected_account_id are written together atomically (services.py:124-125). (3) Every branch carries an explicit, human-readable detail string the agent actually reads and relays: "Reddit integration not found or not fully connected for this tenant." and, for a broken connection, "Reddit connection is no longer valid. Please reconnect in Settings -> Integrations." A modern LLM agent keys off this actionable detail, not the machine "invalid_action" key, so the QA's hypothesized failure mode (agent retries with corrected params instead of prompting reconnect) is speculative and directly contradicted by the detail strings. The "invalid_action" key is a minor code smell at most; no user or operator would experience a defect.

refutation: Not a real defect. It is true that JournalEntryListCreateView (apps/journal/views.py:92-123) does not attach DEPRECATION_HEADERS (defined at lines 62-65) to its GET or POST responses. feature: Legacy daily note entry patch/delete by index
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/journal/daily/<date>/entries/<index>/ updates content/mood/energy on the entry at that index and re-serializes. DELETE removes it. Both return 404 if the note or index is out of range; Deprecated headers emitted. Requires IsAuthenticated.
original_claim: When the entry index is out of range, the view returns Response({"error": ...}, status=404) rather than raise Http404. DRF's exception handler is bypassed, so exception middleware and any 404 interceptors see a plain 200-class JSON response with status=404 set at the transport level. This is a deprecated path, but the inconsistency could confuse clients that check for a DRF-formatted 404 error shape.
refutation: False positive resting on a misreading of DRF response semantics. At apps/journal/views.py:233 and :261 the view returns Response({"error": "Entry index out of range."}, status=404). In Django/DRF, the status argument to Response() IS the transport-level HTTP status — the response goes out as HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found. There is no "200-class response with status=404" as the claim asserts; clients and middleware see a genuine 404. Returning a custom JSON error body with a 404 status is a standard, intended DRF pattern, functionally equivalent (from the client's perspective) to raise Http404. The only real difference is the error-body shape ({"error": ...} vs Django/DRF's default {"detail": ...}), a minor cosmetic/stylistic inconsistency, not a behavioral defect. No real user or operator experiences any malfunction: an out-of-range index correctly yields a 404 with an informative body. The endpoint is additionally explicitly deprecated (docstring: "Use DailyNoteSectionView instead"). The same view already mixes raise Http404 (lines 229, 257) and Response(status=404) (233, 261) with no functional consequence.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/journal/extractions/<uuid>/approve/ looks up a PendingExtraction in status=PENDING scoped to the tenant. Routes to the appropriate approval callback (_approve_goal, _approve_task, _approve_lesson). Sets status=APPROVED and resolved_at=now. Returns {id, status: 'approved'} on 200; 404 if not found or already processed; 500 if the approval callback raises. Requires IsAuthenticated.
original_claim: When a LESSON extraction is approved via the web endpoint, the returned lesson_id from _approve_lesson() is discarded (line 561 ignores the return value). The subsequent pending.save(update_fields=["status", "resolved_at"]) does not include lesson_id, so pending.lesson_id remains NULL. The undo path in _undo_lesson() checks "if pending.lesson_id:" and silently does nothing when it is NULL, meaning any lesson approved through the web UI cannot be undone via the Telegram undo button.
refutation:
The code asymmetry the QA flagged is real but UNREACHABLE as a user-experienced defect, so the claimed impact does not occur. Verified facts: (1) extraction_callbacks.py:61-95 — _approve_lesson() returns (message, lesson.id) but, unlike _approve_goal/_approve_task which set pending.goal/pending.task FK inside the helper, it does NOT persist lesson_id itself; it relies on the caller to do so. (2) views.py:561 — ExtractionApproveView (web) discards the return value and saves only ["status","resolved_at"], leaving pending.lesson_id NULL. The Telegram (extraction_callbacks.py:304-306), LINE (line_webhook.py:1453-1455), and auto-add (extraction.py:674-677) callers all correctly capture and persist lesson_id. So the web handler IS the lone outlier — that part of the claim is accurate. BUT the user-impact premise ("any lesson approved through the web UI cannot be undone") is false, because a lesson can never be approved through the web UI by a real user: the only web surface for pending extractions is the horizons page, whose backend (dashboard/views.py:253-255) filters kind__in=[GOAL, TASK] and excludes LESSON entirely, and the frontend card (frontend/components/pending-goal.tsx) only renders/approves Goal/Task ("Accept Goal"/"Accept Task"). Lessons are auto-approved at extraction time via the extraction.py path (which persists lesson_id correctly), and the Telegram/LINE undo buttons only attach to those already-correct rows. There is no product path where a lesson reaches ExtractionApproveView and then is presented a live undo button, so no operator/user experiences the silent no-op undo. It is a latent, unreachable code inconsistency — defensible to harden, but not a live data-integrity defect.</confirmed_detail>
<suggested_fix">Optional defensive hardening only (no current user impact): make ExtractionApproveView symmetric with the other three callers — capture the return, e.g. _, lesson_id = _approve_lesson(pending); if lesson_id: pending.lesson_id = lesson_id; and add "lesson_id" to update_fields. Better still, move the lesson_id persistence inside _approve_lesson() itself (mirroring how _approve_goal/_approve_task self-persist their FK) so no caller can ever drop it again.</suggested_fix>

expected_behaviour: Called via Telegram/LINE callback router when the user taps an 'Undo' button (callback_data='task_action:undo:<id>'). undo_task_action(pending) restores the task/goal's status and timestamps from before_state. For SUBTASK_CREATE, deletes the created subtask and clears the FK before deleting to avoid CASCADE corruption. Sets PendingTaskAction.status=UNDONE and resolved_at=now. Returns True on success, False if the referenced object no longer exists.
original_claim: For TASK_COMPLETE/PROGRESS/SKIP/DEFER and GOAL_ACHIEVE/ABANDON action kinds, undo_task_action() reverts the referenced Task/Goal row and returns True, but never sets pending.status=UNDONE or pending.resolved_at on the PendingTaskAction itself. The callers (handle_task_action_callback and handle_task_action_postback_line) do this after undo_task_action returns True, so the end result is correct. However, the SUBTASK_CREATE path sets status/resolved_at inside undo_task_action before returning (for CASCADE safety), creating an asymmetry: if a future caller fails to set UNDONE after a True return, task/goal undo PendingTaskActions will remain permanently APPLIED in the database with no resolved_at, allowing the user to tap Undo a second time (the callbacks check status==APPLIED before calling undo_task_action, so the second tap just re-reverts a row that is already reverted). The current callers handle this correctly, but the contract is not enforced by the function itself.
refutation: Not a defect. The verdict describes a hypothetical future-caller risk, not behavior any user/operator experiences. The asymmetry is real but intentional and documented: undo_task_action() sets pending.status=UNDONE/resolved_at internally only for SUBTASK_CREATE (reconciliation.py:333-341) because PendingTaskAction.task is on_delete=CASCADE, so the cascade would wipe the audit row and break the caller's subsequent save(update_fields=...) — explained in the comment at reconciliation.py:328-332. The TASK_COMPLETE/PROGRESS/SKIP/DEFER (344-362) and GOAL_ACHIEVE/ABANDON (364-380) paths have no such FK-ordering constraint, so resolving the PendingTaskAction is deliberately left to the caller. Both — and the only — production callers set status=UNDONE + resolved_at after a True return (task_action_callbacks.py:106-108 and 150-152). Both also guard against double-undo: they short-circuit on status==UNDONE (lines 86-87 / 130-131) and refuse unless status==APPLIED (89-90 / 133-134), so a second tap on a real Telegram/LINE button never re-reverts an already-reverted row. grep confirms no other callers exist (only these two plus tests). The double-revert scenario requires a future caller to violate the contract, which no current code does. No user-facing or data-integrity impact today.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/workspaces/ returns all workspaces ordered by -is_default, -last_used_at, name, plus active_workspace_id and limit (4). POST /api/v1/workspaces/ validates name (required, <= 60 chars, not a reserved prefix), auto-creates a default workspace if this is the first, enforces 4-workspace limit (409 on overflow), generates a unique slug, creates the workspace with description_embedding (pgvector via OpenAI), sets it as the tenant's active workspace, returns 201. Requires IsAuthenticated.
original_claim: The first-create flow (is_first_create check → ensure_default_workspace → count check → workspace.create) is not wrapped in a transaction with select_for_update. Two concurrent POST requests for the same tenant that both arrive when no workspaces exist will both call ensure_default_workspace, which has no DB-level unique constraint on is_default=True, resulting in two default workspaces being created. In practice the single-user-per-tenant model makes this unlikely, but the invariant is not enforced at the DB layer.
refutation: False positive. The claimed data-integrity defect (two concurrent first-create POSTs producing two default workspaces because there is no DB-level constraint on is_default=True) cannot occur. The default workspace is always created with a FIXED slug "general" (DEFAULT_WORKSPACE_SLUG, workspace_services.py:22, used at :111), and the Workspace model enforces unique_together = [("tenant", "slug")] (models.py:570). In the race the QA describes, both threads pass the ensure_default_workspace guard and both attempt Workspace.objects.create(slug="general", is_default=True); the first commits and the second is rejected by Postgres with an IntegrityError on the (tenant, "general") unique constraint. So the uniqueness invariant IS enforced at the DB layer, via the slug rather than the is_default flag. Duplicate default workspaces are impossible. The only theoretical residue is an uncaught IntegrityError to HTTP 500 on the losing thread of a genuinely simultaneous first-create, but (a) that is a different bug from the one claimed and is not a data-integrity issue, and (b) it is effectively untriggerable: this is a single-user-per-tenant, JWT-authed console (user.tenant, workspace_views.py:37), so one user firing two perfectly concurrent first-create requests is not realistic. A user-created workspace also cannot collide because generate_unique_slug appends -2/-3 on any slug collision (workspace_services.py:70-72). No real user or operator would experience the described defect.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/integrations/runtime/<tenant_id>/journal/search/ uses internal-key auth. Query params: q (required, websearch-mode), kind (optional, filters Document.kind), limit (int, default 20, max 50). Returns 400 if q is empty. Uses Postgres full-text search with SearchVector('title', weight='A') + SearchVector('markdown', weight='B') and SearchQuery(search_type='websearch'). Returns documents ranked by SearchRank, filtered to rank > 0.0. Each result includes kind, slug, title, snippet (300-char extract around first match term), updated_at, rank. Returns {query, count, results} with 200.
original_claim: In `RuntimeJournalSearchView.get`, the `limit` parameter is parsed with a bare `int()` call on line 1995 with no try/except guard. Passing a non-integer value such as `?limit=abc` raises an uncaught `ValueError` that DRF's default exception handler does not convert to a 400 response, producing a 500 instead. The expected behaviour states a 400 should be returned on invalid limit. Every comparable view in the same file (`RuntimeSessionsPendingView`) uses the `_parse_positive_int` helper which includes proper error handling.
refutation: The code observation is accurate: RuntimeJournalSearchView.get parses limit with a bare int(request.query_params.get('limit', '20')) at runtime_views.py:1995 with no try/except, and DRF's default exception handler only converts APIException/Http404/PermissionDenied, so a ?limit=abc ValueError would surface as a 500 rather than a 400. But this is NOT a real user/operator-facing defect for three reasons. expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/tokens/create/ requires JWT authentication. Throttled at 10/hour per user (UserPATMintHourThrottle). Body: {name (max 255, required), scopes (list, optional, defaults to ['sessions:write','sessions:read']), expires_in_days (int, optional, 1-365)}. Validates scopes against ALLOWED_PAT_SCOPES = {'sessions:write', 'sessions:read'} — unknown scopes return 400. Generates a raw token (pat_<43-char base64url>), stores SHA-256 hash only (raw token never stored). Returns 201 with {id, name, token (raw, shown once), token_prefix, scopes, expires_at, created_at, warning}.
original_claim: Passing `scopes=[]` (an explicit empty list) silently applies the default full scopes instead of creating a zero-scope token or returning 400. At pat_views.py:73, `data.get('scopes') or list(DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES)` treats `[]` as falsy and substitutes the defaults. A caller who deliberately wants to create a restricted no-scope token — or who passes `[]` expecting a validation error — gets a silently expanded token with both default scopes. The spec documents the default-when-omitted behaviour but does not address the empty-list case.
refutation: Not a real defect. Three independent reasons confirm the `data.get('scopes') or list(DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES)` substitution at pat_views.py:73 is intended and harmless. (1) The serializer field `scopes = ListField(..., required=False, default=list)` (pat_views.py:18) makes an OMITTED field and an explicit `[]` indistinguishable in validated_data — both arrive as `[]`. The spec's documented contract ("scopes optional, defaults to ['sessions:write','sessions:read']") is implemented precisely BY this `or` substitution; there is no downstream way to separate the two cases, and that is by design. (2) A zero-scope PAT is functionally useless: scope enforcement at permissions.py:31 does `self.required_scope in (pat.scopes or [])`, so a `scopes=[]` token would fail BOTH scoped endpoints. No caller has a legitimate reason to deliberately create a token that authorizes nothing — the PAT's entire purpose is to authorize an external app (e.g. YardTalk) to read/write session data. (3) No "silent expansion" risk exists: ALLOWED_PAT_SCOPES == DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES == {'sessions:write','sessions:read'} (permissions.py:13, pat_views.py:13), so defaulting grants exactly the only two scopes that exist, not a broader privilege. The "caller passes [] expecting a 400" premise is also unsupported: the spec only promises 400 for UNKNOWN scopes (validate_scopes, pat_views.py:21-25), and `[]` contains none. No real subscriber or external-app integrator would experience this as a defect.

expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/lessons/pending/ — IsAuthenticated required. Returns all tenant lessons with status='pending'. No pagination. Returns 200 with LessonSerializer array. Same result as GET /api/v1/lessons/?status=pending but via explicit action endpoint.
original_claim: The `pending()` action applies `.filter(status='pending')` on the result of `get_queryset()`, which already filters `status='pending'` when `self.action == 'pending'`. The redundant double-filter is harmless but indicates the action branch in `get_queryset` is dead code for this path.
refutation: Not a defect. The observation is factually accurate but has zero functional impact. In `get_queryset` (apps/lessons/views.py:49-63), status filtering applies only for actions `list`, `pending`, and `galaxy`. When the `pending` endpoint is hit, DRF sets `self.action == "pending"` (the @action method name), so the `elif self.action == "pending": qs = qs.filter(status="pending")` branch at line 58-59 DOES execute — it is not dead code. The `pending()` action at line 132 then applies a second `.filter(status="pending")`. This produces SQL like `WHERE status='pending' AND status='pending'`, which is logically identical to a single filter — no wrong results, no extra rows, no measurable performance cost, no user- or operator-visible behavior change. The QA's secondary characterization ("the action branch in get_queryset is dead code for this path") is itself a misread: that branch is exactly what fires for this path. The only thing present is a harmless cosmetic redundancy (the explicit re-filter in the action method), which the QA itself concedes is "harmless." A real user or operator experiences nothing — correct pending lessons are returned. This does not rise to a logic defect.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/lessons/{pk}/tutor/message/ — IsAuthenticated required. Body: {message (1-5000 chars, required), action: 'continue'|'skip'|'end'}. If action='end', delegates to end_tutoring. If action='skip' or message matches skip phrases (skip/let's move on/next/move on), advances phase locally. Calls LLM with full message history. LLM response (JSON) captures honest signals: restated_accurately, found_edge_cases, connections_found (validated against real neighbor ids only — hallucinated PKs dropped), connection_text, topic_shifted. Advances phase on phase_complete=true. If mastery_achieved or last phase complete, fires end_tutoring and returns merged result under session_close. Returns {session_id, message, current_phase, phase_index, total_phases, phase_complete, mastery_achieved}. 404 on session_not_found.
original_claim: `TutoringMessageSerializer` has no `session_id` field, yet `tutor_message` does `serializer.validated_data.get('session_id', request.data.get('session_id'))`. The serializer lookup always returns `None`, so `session_id` is unconditionally read from raw `request.data` and is never validated by the serializer (no length check, no format check). The subsequent `if not session_id` guard still protects against a missing key, but any value passes unvalidated.
refutation: The mechanical observation is accurate: TutoringMessageSerializer (serializers.py:177-184) declares only `message` and `action`, so `serializer.validated_data.get("session_id", request.data.get("session_id"))` (views.py:462) always falls through to raw request.data and the serializer never validates session_id. But this is not a defect any user or operator would experience. session_id is a server-generated uuid.uuid4() string (tutoring.py:275) used solely as a Django cache lookup key (_cache_key -> cache.get, tutoring.py:223-229). Every concrete bad input is already handled cleanly: missing/empty -> 400 via the `if not session_id` guard (views.py:463-467); malformed, foreign, or oversized -> _load_state returns None -> continue_tutoring returns error session_not_found -> endpoint returns 404 (views.py:480-481), and end_tutoring degrades gracefully. The absent length/format check adds no protection: a UUID regex would not stop a session-not-found anyway, the value is only ever an f-string key into a namespaced cache (no injection/crash surface), and no security or tenant boundary is crossed. The only real artifact is dead fallback code -- the serializer.validated_data.get prefix is vestigial and could be reduced to request.data.get(session_id). That is a tidiness nit, not a logic/security/data-integrity defect with user impact.

expected_behaviour: apply_star_growth(star) computes stage from persisted counts: score = tutoring_sessions_count*3 + journal_entries.count()*1.5 + connections_out.count(). Stage ladder: proto (score=0, no engagement), ignited (>=1 of any), radiant (sessions>=3 or notes>=3 or connections>=5 or score>=5), supernova (sessions>=8 or notes>=8 or score>=14). Only promotes (STAGE_RANK check). Saves star_stage field if promoted. Called by: end_tutoring, star_journal_create, star connect action.
original_claim: In `end_tutoring`, `tutoring_sessions_count` is incremented with a non-atomic read-modify-write pattern (`star.tutoring_sessions_count += 1; star.save(update_fields=[...])`). If two tutoring sessions for the same star complete concurrently, both reads see the same pre-increment value and one increment is lost, stalling star promotion. The correct fix is `F('tutoring_sessions_count') + 1`. In practice concurrent same-star tutoring is unlikely, but the race window exists.
refutation: The read-modify-write at apps/lessons/tutoring.py:430-433 (star.tutoring_sessions_count += 1; star.save(update_fields=[...])) is technically non-atomic, but no real user/operator would experience this as a defect. end_tutoring is only ever invoked from three interactive DRF view actions (apps/lessons/views.py:473, :485, :500), each keyed to a per-session UUID and driven by a single human one request at a time. Tutoring is an inherently serial, single-user, message-by-message flow; there is no cron, automation, or batch path that fires end_tutoring (confirmed by grep — only callers are the views and the mastery auto-end inside tutor_message). For an increment to be lost, the same person would need two distinct active tutoring sessions on the SAME star ending in the same instant — practically impossible in this UX. Even then the impact is negligible: growth.py's promotion engine is monotonic (never demotes) and threshold-based on a BLENDED score (sessions*3 + notes*1.5 + connections) with sessions>=1/3/8 gates, so the session count is not the sole driver. A single lost increment would at most delay a stage promotion by one session and self-heals on the next tutoring session, note, or connection. No data loss, no incorrect persisted state, no user-visible stall — this is a defense-in-depth code-quality nit, not a defect.

expected_behaviour: Triggered by `provision_tenant(tenant_id)` (called from `provision_tenant_task` via QStash). Requires tenant in PENDING or PROVISIONING status, otherwise logs and returns. Executes an ordered pipeline: (1) generates openclaw.json via `generate_openclaw_config`, runs `audit_config_security` — warnings log to PlatformIssueLog, errors raise and reset tenant to PENDING; (2) creates Azure Managed Identity (`mi-nbhd-<uuid>`); (3) generates a 48-byte `secrets.token_urlsafe` internal API key, writes it to Key Vault as `tenant-<uuid>-internal-key`, stores hash on Tenant; (4) optionally creates a per-tenant OpenRouter sub-key via `create_sub_key` if `OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED` — failure here is non-fatal (falls back to shared key); (5) assigns KV + ACR pull roles to the managed identity; (6) creates Azure File Share `ws-<uuid[:20]>` and registers environment storage; (7) uploads openclaw.json to file share; (8) renders persona workspace files via `render_workspace_files`; (9) creates Container App `oc-<uuid[:20]>` via Azure ARM; (10) updates Tenant row (container_id, container_fqdn, managed_identity_id, container_image_tag, status=ACTIVE, provisioned_at). On any exception in steps 1-10 tenant is reset to PENDING and the exception re-raises. Post-provision (non-critical, do not reset status): sends Telegram welcome if chat_id present; sends Day-0 welcome email; seeds USER.md via `push_user_md(force=True)`; enqueues `seed_cron_jobs` via QStash with 60s delay.
original_claim: The delayed cron seeding step in `provision_tenant` (step 5 / post-provision) always fails silently. It attempts `from apps.cron.views import _schedule_qstash_task` but that function does not exist in `apps/cron/views.py` (zero occurrences confirmed), so the import always raises `ImportError`. The except block then immediately calls `seed_cron_jobs(tenant)` without the intended 60-second delay, which also fails because the newly-created container is not yet ready. Both failures are swallowed as warnings, so newly-provisioned tenants have no cron jobs seeded at provision time. Jobs are only restored later via fleet reconcilers if/when the tenant becomes active.
refutation: False positive on the claimed user impact. TRUE facts: _schedule_qstash_task does not exist in apps/cron/views.py (grep = zero definitions), so the import at services.py:428 always raises ImportError in production, and the except block always runs the fallback seed_cron_jobs(tenant) immediately with no 60s delay. BUT the claimed user impact ("newly-provisioned tenants have no cron jobs seeded at provision time") is wrong. The fallback seed_cron_jobs (services.py:990) branches on tenant.postgres_cron_canonical, whose model default is True (models.py:531-532) and was made universal by migration 0058 (legacy gateway path removed). The postgres-canonical branch (services.py:1016-1050) just upserts CronJob rows into Postgres with ZERO container/gateway interaction — it does NOT require a warm/ready container. So the secondary premise ("also fails because the container is not yet ready") is false: cron rows ARE seeded into Postgres at provision time, and the signal-driven reconciler syncs them to the container SQLite on boot/wake. The 60s delay only ever mattered for the now-dead legacy gateway path. Net functional outcome: cron jobs are correctly seeded at provision time. Residual is code-quality only: the try/import block is dead (always throws), emits a spurious WARNING per provision (cosmetic noise), and the "# TODO: schedule with delay" was never implemented — none of which produces the claimed failure. Tests corroborate the function never existed: every patch uses create=True to force-create the missing attribute (test_services.py:36,97,178).

expected_behaviour: `hibernate_suspended_task()`: queries all tenants with `status=SUSPENDED` and non-empty `container_id`. For each, calls `hibernate_container_app(tenant.container_id)` which deactivates all revisions to 0 replicas. Returns `{hibernated, failed, total}`. Does NOT set `hibernated_at` (that field tracks idle hibernation of ACTIVE tenants; suspended tenants have a separate billing-status path). Management command `hibernate_suspended` supports `--dry-run`.
original_claim: `tenants.count()` at line 91 of tasks.py runs a second database query after the iteration loop has already completed. Because the queryset is re-evaluated, the returned `total` can differ from the number of rows actually iterated if tenants are added or deleted concurrently, making the `total` field in the returned dict potentially misleading to log monitors and callers. No operational failure occurs, but the stats surface is unreliable.
refutation: False positive — the premise that `tenants.count()` issues a second database query is incorrect. The `for tenant in tenants:` loop at line 84 fully evaluates the queryset, populating its `_result_cache`. Django's `QuerySet.count()` short-circuits to `len(self._result_cache)` when the cache is already populated, so the `.count()` at line 91 issues NO SQL and simply returns the count of the already-iterated rows. There is no re-evaluation, no second query, and therefore no concurrency window in which `total` could diverge from the number of rows iterated. `total` is guaranteed to equal `hibernated + failed`. Additionally this is a control-plane hibernation sweep task, not a user-facing surface, further limiting any theoretical impact.

expected_behaviour: `reconcile_welcomes_task()`: daily fleet sweep. Iterates active tenants with containers. For each: calls `_schedule_fuel_welcome(tenant)` if `tenant.fuel_enabled`; calls `_schedule_finance_welcome(tenant)` if `tenant.finance_active`. Tallies `WelcomeStatus` outcomes per feature. Returns `{tenants, fuel: {status_counts}, finance: {status_counts}, statuses}`. The schedulers are self-healing: stale one-shot crons whose fire date already passed are replaced with fresh ones; confirmed-delivered tenants (`Tenant.welcomes_sent[feature]`) are short-circuited. Management command `backfill_welcomes` provides the same behavior on-demand with `--feature` and `--tenant` filters.
original_claim: The automated `reconcile_welcomes_task` gates the finance welcome on `tenant.finance_active` (which applies the global `GRAVITY_ENABLED` kill switch), while the manual `backfill_welcomes` management command gates on `tenant.finance_enabled` directly, bypassing the platform kill switch. With `GRAVITY_ENABLED=False` as the production default, the task never schedules finance welcomes even when the flag is eventually enabled, whereas the command would. The two entry points have inconsistent semantics for the same operation — operators running `backfill_welcomes` will see different behavior than the daily reconcile sweep.
refutation:
The two entry points do gate differently -- reconcile_welcomes_task uses tenant.finance_active (the GRAVITY_ENABLED-aware kill switch, tasks.py:1146) while backfill_welcomes uses tenant.finance_enabled directly (backfill_welcomes.py:68) -- but this is NOT a user/operator-facing defect.

(1) The QA's stated impact is a misread. finance_active evaluates settings.GRAVITY_ENABLED at runtime (models.py:716-718). When GRAVITY_ENABLED is eventually flipped to True, finance_active becomes True for any finance_enabled tenant, and the daily reconcile task DOES then schedule their welcomes within 24h. The claim that the task 'never schedules finance welcomes even when the flag is eventually enabled' is false.

(2) The task's finance_active gating is the CORRECT, intended behavior. GRAVITY_ENABLED is an authoritative platform-wide kill switch that pauses all finance egress/surfacing for privacy (models.py:708-714). Sending a 'welcome to Gravity' message while Gravity is paused would surface a disabled feature -- precisely what the kill switch exists to prevent. A daily watchdog that respects the kill switch is right, not broken.

(3) The only residual is that the manual backfill_welcomes command is slightly more permissive. But the divergence can only manifest if (a) an operator manually runs the command AND (b) tenants have finance_enabled=True while GRAVITY_ENABLED=False -- which the live toggle path actively blocks: FinanceSettingsView PATCH refuses to set finance_enabled=True when GRAVITY_ENABLED is off (finance/views.py:310). So the precondition for the inconsistency is itself prevented in normal operation. This is a low-likelihood, operator-only, defense-in-depth nit where the command (not the task) is the looser one -- not a defect a real user or operator would experience.

feature: Daily Note Backfill from PendingMessages (One-Off Recovery)
expected_behaviour: python manage.py backfill_daily_notes_from_messages --tenant <uuid-or-prefix> --start YYYY-MM-DD --end YYYY-MM-DD [--dry-run]. Resolves tenant by prefix (CommandError on no match or ambiguous). Queries PendingMessage for the tenant and date range (interpreted in tenant's local timezone). Groups by local date. For each day, renders a markdown block (## Replayed conversation — backfilled YYYY-MM-DD, bullet lines at HH:MM local). Downloads existing workspace/memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md (fall through to empty on error), appends the block, uploads back. --dry-run prints preview without writing. Raises CommandError on upload failure after partial writes.
original_claim: The heading in _format_day_block reads '## Replayed conversation — backfilled {today_iso}' where today_iso is the date the command is run, not the date of the messages being replayed. Each day's block is written into its file (e.g. workspace/memory/2026-05-05.md) but the heading inside says the backfill ran on 2026-06-22. When OpenClaw's dreaming lane indexes these files, the heading date mismatches the file's calendar date, which could confuse grounded-backfill attribution.
refutation: Not a defect — intended behavior with a correct label. The heading reads "## Replayed conversation — **backfilled** {today_iso}" (line 91-93). The word "backfilled" denotes when the recovery operation ran, which is exactly what `datetime.now(tz).date()` produces. This is a provenance stamp for the backfill, not a claim about when the conversation occurred. The actual message/conversation date is preserved in two correct places: (1) the file path `workspace/memory/{day.isoformat()}.md` (line 202), which is what OpenClaw's dreaming/memory_search index by, and (2) the per-line local HH:MM timestamps (line 105). The italic body note (lines 95-99) further anchors attribution by saying "Conversation messages from **this date**" — "this date" = the file's calendar date. The `day` parameter is even available in `_format_day_block` (line 70) had they wanted it in the heading; they deliberately chose run-date with an explicit "backfilled" qualifier. This is also a one-off operator recovery command (docstring lines 1-33, built for the May 5–13 canary recovery), not a user-facing surface — no real user or indexer experiences the claimed confusion. The "could confuse grounded-backfill attribution" claim is speculative with no evidence of any consumer that parses the heading date as the conversation date.

expected_behaviour: python manage.py remove_zombie_heartbeats [--dry-run]. Queries ACTIVE tenants with container_id and heartbeat_enabled=False. For each: calls cron.list on the gateway; if 'Heartbeat Check-in' job is found, calls cron.remove with its id/jobId. Skips tenants with no container_fqdn. --dry-run reports what would be removed without writing. Returns removed/skipped/errors counts. Sleeps 1s between tenants to avoid Azure gateway rate limits.
original_claim: The queryset filter uses `container_id__gt=""` but not `container_fqdn__gt=""`. A tenant with a container_id but empty container_fqdn is not pre-filtered; `invoke_gateway_tool` will raise a GatewayError for that tenant and it will be counted as an error instead of a skip. The expected behaviour says 'Skips tenants with no container_fqdn' but the code degrades that skip to a gateway error.
refutation:
Not a real defect. The mechanism is technically accurate: the queryset pre-filters on container_id__gt="" but not container_fqdn__gt="" (remove_zombie_heartbeats.py:40-46), and invoke_gateway_tool raises GatewayError when container_fqdn is empty (gateway_client.py:145-146), which the command catches at line 56-58 and counts as an error rather than a skip. BUT the triggering state (an ACTIVE tenant with container_id set and container_fqdn empty) is not produced anywhere in the codebase. Both fields are written together in the same save() at provisioning (services.py:355-356, with status flipped to ACTIVE in the same block) and cleared together on deprovision (services.py:740-741). No assignment path sets container_id without container_fqdn. The codebase itself treats them as an inseparable pair: services.py:93 uses Q(container_id="") | Q(container_fqdn="") as a single "not fully provisioned" exclusion. Even if the impossible state somehow arose, impact is purely cosmetic for an operator running this ad-hoc command: one tenant is tallied under "errors" (with a clear stderr line) instead of "skipped". No wrong action is taken, nothing breaks, the run still completes. This is an unreachable-state counting cosmetic in a manually-invoked operator tool, not something a real operator would experience.</confirmed_detail>

feature: Send iOS/web chat turn (enqueue to OpenClaw)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/chat/messages/ requires JWT auth. Body: {text, client_msg_id (optional), thread_id (optional)}. text is validated: empty→400, >8000 chars→400. client_msg_id max 64 chars→400 if exceeded; auto-generated UUID hex if absent. Idempotency: if client_msg_id already exists for the tenant, returns the existing AppChatMessage with HTTP 200 (no re-enqueue). Budget gate via check_budget: over-budget tenant creates an AppChatMessage with status=error, error='budget_exhausted', returns 200 (not created=True). Thread resolved from thread_id or defaults to the shared main thread (get_or_create race-safe); invalid thread_id→404. A fresh PendingMessage (channel=ios) is created, user text is decorated with datetime context + chat-context marker, then enqueued to QStash drain task. Returns 201 on new turn, 200 on replay. tenant.first_message_at stamped on first-ever message (conditional UPDATE, race-safe). Cache-Control: no-store on all responses.
original_claim: first_message_at is stamped inside enqueue_message_for_tenant (pending_queue.py:381), which is only reached when the turn is both new and not budget-exhausted. In the budget-exhausted branch of enqueue_tenant_turn (chat_views.py:183-197), enqueue_message_for_tenant is never called, so an over-budget iOS tenant's very first-ever message does not stamp first_message_at. Telegram/LINE are unaffected because their budget check fires downstream (inside the drain task), after enqueue_message_for_tenant has already stamped the field. The practical impact is that a user who is over-budget the instant they first try the iOS app is invisible to onboarding-drop-off cohort tracking.
refutation: False positive. The finding's central claim — an iOS-specific asymmetry where Telegram/LINE stamp first_message_at but iOS does not on an over-budget first message — is a misread. first_message_at is written in exactly ONE place: inside enqueue_message_for_tenant (pending_queue.py:381). On EVERY channel the budget check fires upstream of that enqueue and returns early when over-budget: iOS in enqueue_tenant_turn (chat_views.py:182-197, returns before the enqueue at line 219), the Telegram poller (check_budget at poller.py:868 returns before the enqueue at poller.py:1370), and the LINE webhook (check_budget at line_webhook.py:1038 returns before the enqueue at line_webhook.py:1256). So an over-budget first-ever message skips the stamp identically on all three channels — there is no iOS-only defect. Additionally, the residual gap is practically unreachable as an onboarding-cohort concern: check_budget (billing/services.py:256-293) only blocks when the tenant has zero spendable budget (allowance exhausted AND no credit) or the global platform budget is exhausted. A freshly-provisioned tenant on a trial/plan has spendable budget, so a normal new user's first message stamps first_message_at correctly. Being over-budget on the literal first-ever message requires platform-wide global-budget exhaustion (not a per-tenant activation signal) or a zero-budget provisioning corner case. The behavior is intended and uniform (gate before doing work) with no realistic onboarding-tracking impact.

feature: Siri respond — fast model with escalation to full agent
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/siri/respond/ — JWT auth, per-minute throttle (SiriRespondMinuteThrottle). Body: {intent (required, max 2048 chars), client_msg_id (optional)}. Empty intent→400. Renders context snapshot (rehydrated). Calls a fast model (chat_completion, 8s timeout, max 300 tokens) with the intent + snapshot. If model returns [[ESCALATE]] sentinel → escalates to full agent via enqueue_tenant_turn (same path as ChatMessageView POST). Returns {answered:true, text:...} or {answered:false, escalated:true, client_msg_id:..., turn:{...}}. Model failure → silently escalates. Escalated turn client polls GET /api/v1/chat/messages/<client_msg_id>/ for the reply.
original_claim: The fast-answer success response is missing the documented 'model' field. The class docstring at line 143 promises '{"answered": true, "text": "...", "model": "<id>"}', but the actual response at line 172 returns only {"answered": True, "escalated": False, "text": answer}. The resolved model is captured in the local variable '_model' (line 191) but then discarded. Any iOS client code that reads 'model' to display which fast model answered will receive undefined. Separately, _MAX_ASK_CHARS is 1000 (line 50) but the spec says 2048; the tighter limit is not harmful but is undocumented.
refutation: False positive. The only place a `model` field is "promised" is the class docstring (siri_views.py:143). The authoritative contract disagrees with that docstring and agrees with the code: the FA-0956 record's expected_behaviour documents the success response as {answered:true, text:...} with NO `model` field, and the test suite (test_siri_views.py:111-113, 194-196) asserts only `answered` and `text` — never `model`. The wire format clients actually receive (line 172: {answered, escalated, text}) matches the documented contract and the tests; `model` was never part of the real contract. The claim that iOS client code reading `model` will receive undefined is speculative — there is no evidence in this repo's contract or tests that any client reads it, and a docstring is never seen by a user or operator, so there is no user-facing impact. At worst this is a stale/typo docstring. The secondary _MAX_ASK_CHARS=1000-vs-spec-2048 point is conceded by the reviewer as "not harmful"; it is an intentional defensive guard with explicit inline rationale (lines 48-49: voice utterances are short, a longer payload is misuse/paste), so it is intended behaviour, not a defect.

expected_behaviour: _looks_like_openrouter_credit_limit() checks for HTTP 402, or 4xx/5xx body containing 'insufficient credit', 'credit_limit', 'quota exceeded' etc. (case-insensitive). _handle_openrouter_credit_limit(): if tenant is budget-exempt OR has purchased_credit > 0 → raises the OR key ceiling via sync_or_key_limit() and returns (no hibernation). Otherwise: bumps estimated_cost_this_month to effective_cost_budget cap so check_budget fires on next inbound, hibernates container via _hibernate_for_quota, sends branded cap-exhausted email (idempotent sent-at marker), sends channel-native budget-exhausted message to user (localized). For iOS turns: AppChatMessage flipped to ERROR. Returns False (not a liveness signal) from the drain helper.
original_claim: _looks_like_openrouter_credit_limit() does not detect HTTP 200 responses that contain a credit-limit error string in the body. The code at line 174 gates the needle check on '400 <= status < 600', so a 200 from OpenClaw 5.7 wrapping an upstream OR credit-limit error (a case explicitly documented in the code comment at line 125 and in the expected behaviour) returns False. The circuit breaker then does not fire: raise_for_status() is a no-op on 200, _extract_ai_response() returns None (no 'choices' key in an error body), and the user sees a silent empty-response error instead of a budget-exhausted message and hibernation. The tenant continues to accumulate charges until the next 4xx/402 hits.
refutation: False positive. The 200 exclusion at line 174 (the 400<=status<600 gate) is intentional, tested, and matches the feature spec. Three independent sources confirm the real contract is HTTP 402 or 4xx/5xx body containing a credit-limit needle, NOT 200: (a) the feature record's own expected_behaviour says "HTTP 402, or 4xx/5xx body containing insufficient credit"; (b) apps/router/test_openrouter_credit_limit.py:40 test_200_with_unrelated_error_does_not_match explicitly codifies that a 200-with-error-body returns False; (c) the conservatism note at lines 163-164 (false positives would cause spurious hibernation) deliberately restricts detection to 4xx/5xx. The QA claim hinges on OpenClaw 5.7 returning an HTTP 200 wrapping an upstream OpenRouter credit-limit error string, but there is no code or upstream evidence of this happening. The 200 path is reserved for the gateway can't-reach-model case, which emits the distinct _GATEWAY_ERROR_STRINGS ("No response from OpenClaw"), handled cleanly by _extract_ai_response (returns None, the documented empty-response behaviour, not a silent crash or runaway charging). An OpenRouter credit rejection is an upstream HTTP error (402/429) that OpenClaw wraps in its internal-error envelope as a 5xx (the team models this with test_500_with_generic_internal_error at line 43), and a leaked credit-limit message in that 5xx IS caught by the existing gate. Furthermore, matching credit-limit needles inside a genuine 200 success body would be actively harmful: a successful assistant reply that merely discusses the user's credit limit or quota would trigger spurious hibernation, exactly the false positive the code guards against. The only genuine (cosmetic) issue is the docstring at lines 159-161 inaccurately listing "HTTP 200 / 5xx" as detection source 3, contradicting both the code and the spec, a documentation nit, not a logic/UX/data defect.

expected_behaviour: Handles LINE postback events with data starting 'gate_approve:' or 'gate_deny:'. Parses action_id from data. Fetches PendingAction scoped to the tenant. If action not in PENDING status, returns without effect. If is_expired, sets status=EXPIRED, logs ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message. Otherwise sets status=APPROVED or DENIED, records responded_at, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message. Errors (action not found, parse failure) are logged. No response message is sent to the user — update_gate_message handles the LINE-side update.
original_claim: When action.is_expired is True, `action.save(update_fields=["status"])` writes EXPIRED but `responded_at` is never set, leaving it null for an action the user technically interacted with. The ActionAuditLog for the expired case also omits `responded_at`, inconsistent with the approve/deny path. No user-visible breakage but the audit trail is incomplete for expired actions.
refutation: False positive — the omission of `responded_at` on the expired path is intentional, semantically correct, and consistent system-wide. The defect's premise (an expired action is "one the user technically interacted with") is a misread: `PendingAction.is_expired` (models.py:80-81) is purely time-based (`timezone.now() > expires_at and status == PENDING`) and fires whenever the action's deadline has passed, independent of any user tap. An expired action was, by definition, never validly responded to, so `responded_at = null` is the correct value — `responded_at` means "when the user actually responded to the gate," which never happened. The audit log's `responded_at` column is explicitly nullable (models.py:122, `null=True`) and the EXPIRED `result` is itself the record that the action lapsed without a response. This exact pattern (set status=EXPIRED, save update_fields=["status"] only, create ActionAuditLog with no responded_at) is identical across all five handling sites: line_webhook.py:1338-1349, poller.py:1514-1523, views.py:213-223, views.py:285-293, and the canonical expiry cron tasks.py:28-38. The approve/deny path correctly DOES set responded_at because the user genuinely responded. There is no inconsistency and no user- or operator-visible impact.

expected_behaviour:
_extract_line_reply_context checks message.quotedMessageId on inbound events. If present, looks up LineOutboundMessage by (tenant, line_message_id). If found with text_excerpt: prepends '[Replying to: "<excerpt up to 200 chars>"]

'. If found but excerpt is empty (row predates feature): prepends '[Replying to an earlier message of yours]

'. If not found (pruned, >30 days old): returns empty string (silently drops context). This prefix is inserted before the user's message_text in the PendingMessage payload. original_claim: The expected behaviour states that a not-found quoted message ID (pruned row, >30 days old) returns an empty string (silently drops context). The actual code at line 441 collapses 'row is None' and 'row found but text_excerpt is empty' into the same branch, returning '[Replying to an earlier message of yours]\n\n' for both. The code comment explains this is intentional ('Still annotate so the agent knows the user is replying to something'), which is arguably better behaviour than silent drop, but it diverges from the inventory specification. Impact is benign (agent gets slightly more context than specified) but the mismatch may cause confusion if the behaviour is relied upon in downstream tests or clients.
refutation: Not a real defect. The code at line 441 (`if not row or not row.text_excerpt:`) deliberately treats both "quoted row not found / pruned >30d" and "row found but text_excerpt empty (predates feature)" identically, returning '[Replying to an earlier message of yours]\n\n'. An inline comment (lines 442-446) explicitly documents this as intentional: annotate so the agent knows the user is replying to something it previously said. The only divergence is against one sentence in the feature record's expected_behaviour ("If not found... returns empty string"), and that sentence even contradicts the same record's inventory_notes ("references to messages older than 30 days show the 'earlier message' fallback"), which accurately describes the actual code. So the spec is internally self-contradictory and the code matches the inventory_notes, not a bug. User impact is neutral-to-positive: a LINE subscriber quote-replying to a pruned message gives the agent a neutral "replying to something I said" marker (no fabricated content) instead of silently dropping all reply context, which better serves the feature's own user story. No downstream test or client is cited as relying on the empty-string behavior, and the claim itself concedes the impact is benign. Intended, code-documented behavior; the spec line is stale, not the implementation.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/tenants/line/unlink/ — requires IsAuthenticated. Calls unlink_line: if user.line_user_id is None, returns False → 400 'LINE not linked.' original_claim: When a user unlinks LINE, the unlink_line service unconditionally sets preferred_channel='telegram' even if telegram_chat_id is not set (line_service.py lines 163-165). For LINE-only users (no Telegram, no iOS device) who unlink LINE, this leaves them with preferred_channel='telegram' but no working delivery channel. resolve_user_channel will return None and CronDeliveryView will respond 422. The inventory_notes acknowledges this but flags it as a known asymmetry rather than a tracked defect. Proactive cron messages silently fail with 422 until the user reconnects a channel.
refutation: False positive. The claim is that resetting preferred_channel to 'telegram' on LINE unlink harms a LINE-only user (no Telegram, no iOS device), causing cron deliveries to 422. But the reset is cosmetic and does NOT change the outcome. After unlink_line runs, user.line_user_id is None. In resolve_user_channel (cron_delivery.py:48-83) the value of preferred_channel is irrelevant for such a user: the preferred=='line' branch (line 66) can never match because line_user_id is None, and the preferred=='telegram' branch (line 68) can never match because telegram_chat_id is None. The fallback (lines 72-75) fails for both, and the DeviceToken check (line 80) fails when there is no device. So resolve_user_channel returns None whether preferred_channel is left as 'line' or reset to 'telegram' — the 422 is produced by the user genuinely having zero delivery surfaces, not by the channel reset. A user who disconnected LINE, never had Telegram, and has no iOS device legitimately has nowhere to deliver to; the 422 'no_channel_linked' is the correct, deliberate response with a clear error body (cron_delivery.py:145-152), not a silent failure. The reset to 'telegram' is a harmless default that only becomes meaningful (and correct) once the user reconnects Telegram. The inventory_notes already characterizes this as a benign asymmetry. No real user experiences a worse outcome than the unavoidable no-channel state they put themselves in.

feature: Siri Fast Responder with Escalation (Tier 2 / Tier 3)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/siri/respond/ requires JWT auth. Body: {"intent": "...", "client_msg_id": "<optional>"}. Validates: non-empty intent (400 empty_intent), intent <= 1000 chars (400 intent_too_long), client_msg_id <= 64 chars (400 invalid_client_msg_id). Builds a 4000-char PII-rehydrated snapshot, then calls chat_completion with platform OpenRouter ZDR key using models [deepseek-v4-flash, deepseek-v4-pro] at 8s timeout / 300 max tokens. If the fast model replies without the [[ESCALATE]] sentinel, returns {"answered": true, "text": "<spoken answer>"} with no persistence. If the model returns [[ESCALATE]] (leading, case-insensitive), is empty, or throws, escalates: creates AppChatMessage + PendingMessage via enqueue_tenant_turn (budget-gated via check_budget — over-budget turns get status=error/error=budget_exhausted and no container wake), returns {"answered": false, "escalated": true, "client_msg_id": "<id>", "turn": {...}}. Escalation is idempotent on client_msg_id. Rate-limited at 30/minute (SiriRespondMinuteThrottle). All responses have Cache-Control: no-store.
original_claim: Two defects. (1) The fast-path success response at siri_views.py:172 returns {"answered": true, "escalated": false, "text": "..."} but omits the "model" key specified by the expected behaviour — the model ID returned by chat_completion() is discarded via `_model` at line 191 and never included in the response, so the iOS client cannot know which model answered. (2) The client_msg_id > 64 char validation (siri_views.py:215) lives inside _escalate(), which is only reached after the model call completes — a request with an oversized client_msg_id that requires escalation silently burns a full LLM round-trip before returning 400, instead of failing fast before the model call as the spec implies.
refutation:
Both claimed defects are false positives.

(1) Missing "model" key — MISREAD. The authoritative expected_behaviour for FA-0998 states the fast-path success response is {"answered": true, "text": "<spoken answer>"} with NO "model" key. The code at siri_views.py:172 returns exactly that ({"answered": True, "escalated": False, "text": answer}), matching the spec. The "model" key appears only in an aspirational docstring example at line 143 that diverges from both the spec and the implementation; discarding the model id via _model (line 191) is intended. No iOS client behaviour described in the spec depends on a "model" field. Not a defect.

(2) client_msg_id > 64 validation running after the model call — negligible ordering nit, not a medium logic defect. feature: Budget check and container hibernation on quota exhaustion
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller call check_budget(tenant) before waking/forwarding. If budget_reason is non-empty, _hibernate_for_quota(tenant) is called: if container is not already hibernated, calls azure_client.hibernate_container_app(container_id), sets Tenant.hibernated_at=now, and calls send_cost_exhausted_email (idempotent via cost_exhausted_email_sent_at). Then _build_budget_exhausted_message / _send_budget_exhausted is called, returning a localized message. Global budget (reason='global') uses 'budget_unavailable' copy; per-tenant uses 'budget_exhausted_trial' or 'budget_exhausted_paid' with a billing URL. Budget-exempt tenants and tenants with purchased_credit skip hibernation via _handle_openrouter_credit_limit.
original_claim: The budget-exhausted billing URL in _build_budget_exhausted_message (webhook) and _send_budget_exhausted (poller) routes to {frontend_url}/billing, while the suspended-tenant path routes to {frontend_url}/settings/billing. The expected behaviour document says /settings/billing. If these are distinct frontend routes, users hitting the budget cap are linked to the wrong page.
refutation: False positive. The budget-exhausted messages do link to {frontend_url}/billing (apps/router/views.py:161 and apps/router/poller.py:1461) while the suspended path links to {frontend_url}/settings/billing (apps/router/views.py:267), so the two backend strings genuinely differ. BUT /billing is not a wrong or dead route: frontend/app/billing/page.tsx is a 12-line BillingRedirect client component whose entire body is router.replace("/settings/billing"). So a user who taps the budget-cap link lands on /billing and is immediately forwarded to the exact same /settings/billing page the suspended path uses. Both backend paths funnel the user to the identical destination. Both routes are authenticated (neither is in app-shell.tsx publicPages), which is correct because budget-exhausted users are logged-in subscribers. The only observable difference is one instant client-side redirect hop (a momentary return-null render) — no broken navigation, no wrong page, no stranded user. The feature's expected_behaviour only requires "a billing URL" for the budget copy; it does not mandate the literal /settings/billing for budget messages. /billing is a deliberate alias/redirect, so a real user does not experience this as a defect.

expected_behaviour: _send_apology_for_dropped_pending_message is called when a PendingMessage reaches _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3. _send_apology_for_stale_pending_message is called when the head row is older than _STALE_MESSAGE_AGE_SECONDS=600s. Both use localized error_msg templates (dropped_message/dropped_message_with_excerpt or stale_message/stale_message_with_excerpt). For Telegram, sends via httpx POST to sendMessage. user_text is sanitized by strip_internal_framing to remove [System:...]/[Now:...]/[chat:...] framing before excerpting (max 50 chars). Age is formatted as Nm or ~Nh. iOS turns instead flip AppChatMessage status to ERROR and fire notify_app_reply_error APNs push.
original_claim: _send_apology_for_stale_pending_message (line 1026) builds the excerpt directly from msg.user_text without calling strip_internal_framing, unlike _send_apology_for_dropped_pending_message (line 1110) which does. If user_text ever contains [System: …], [Now: …], or [chat: …] framing (e.g. forwarded from the container-update or restart context-wrapping paths), those markers would be visible to the user in the stale apology excerpt.
refutation:
The code asymmetry is factually accurate but is NOT a user-facing defect. Line 1026 (_send_apology_for_stale_pending_message) builds the excerpt directly from msg.user_text, while line 1110 (_send_apology_for_dropped_pending_message) runs it through strip_internal_framing. However, PendingMessage.user_text is clean by construction at EVERY call site, so the strip is unreachable-in-practice belt-and-suspenders — the dropped variant's own comment (lines 1106-1109) explicitly labels its strip as "Defense in depth: even though every call site is supposed to pass raw_user_text so PendingMessage.user_text is clean."

The claim hypothesizes that framing leaks in "from the container-update or restart context-wrapping paths." Those are exactly the paths the code already handles: poller.py:795-797 (container update), :1269-1273 (restart), and :831-835 (button tap) build a framed context_msg (`[System: just updated…]`, `[System: assistant was restarting…]`, `[User tapped button: …]`) but pass an EXPLICIT clean raw_user_text= argument, with comments stating this is so "the dropped-message apology quotes the user's actual words instead of the framing." The framed text goes into payload.message_text (sent to the agent), never into user_text.

For all other paths, _forward_to_container (poller.py:1314) and the LINE equivalent (line_webhook.py:1210) capture raw_user_text = message_text BEFORE the [Now:]/[chat:] datetime/chat markers are prepended (poller.py:1351), so the stored user_text never carries those markers. The iOS chat_views path (chat_views.py:230) also passes clean text, and the stale function returns early for iOS (line 1020-1022) before ever reaching the excerpt code. No call site stores [System:]/[Now:]/[chat:] framing into user_text. Therefore a real LINE/Telegram user would never see framing markers in the stale apology excerpt.

This is a latent code-cleanliness/consistency gap (a future call site that forgot to pass clean raw_user_text would leak only in the stale path, not the dropped path), but it is not a defect any current user experiences.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/password-reset/request/ is AllowAny. Accepts {email}. Applies dual rate-limiting: per-email (3 req/hr) and per-IP (5 req/hr) using Django cache; cache unavailability fails open. Regardless of whether the user exists, always returns 200 with a constant message to prevent account enumeration. If user exists and is_active, generates a uid (base64-encoded PK) and Django's default_token_generator token, builds a reset URL pointing to FRONTEND_URL/reset-password?uid=&token=, and sends a multipart HTML+text email via Mailgun. Email failures are swallowed (logged only). Returns 429 if rate limit exceeded.
original_claim: The per-IP rate limit counter is only incremented when the per-email check passes (line 101 is not reached when line 99 returns 429). An attacker who cycles through more than 3 email addresses can keep hitting the email rate-limit for each without accumulating IP counts, effectively bypassing the per-IP cap for those excess attempts. Additionally, the `cache.get + cache.set` sequence is non-atomic (lines 50-51); under concurrent requests, multiple threads can read the same count and both write count+1, allowing up to 2× the intended burst through. Both issues are low-severity given the small window and fail-open design intent.
refutation:
Not a meaningful defect; both mechanical observations are accurate but neither produces the claimed per-IP bypass.

Claim 1 (per-IP cap bypass): True that _rate_limited increments-and-checks, and that when the per-email check (line 99) returns 429 the per-IP increment (line 101) is skipped. But the ONLY requests that skip the IP increment are ones already blocked by the per-email limit -- the 4th+ request for the same email, which sends no reset email and accomplishes nothing for the attacker. Every request that actually does work (the first 3 per email, passing at email counts 1/2/3 <= 3) reaches line 101 and increments the IP counter normally. Trace: 3 effective sends for email A bring IP to 3, then 1 each for emails B and C bring IP to 5, and the next effective request trips the per-IP 429. So the per-IP cap of 5 still fully bounds the number of effective (email-sending) requests per IP per hour. The 'excess' requests that escape IP counting are exactly the no-op 429'd ones, so there is no real amplification, enumeration, or DoS benefit, and the ordering matches the in-code comment's stated intent (both buckets checked to stop email- and IP-pivoting).

Claim 2 (non-atomic get+set race): Genuinely a read-modify-write race that could let a few extra requests slip through under exact concurrency, but this is a well-known low-consequence property of cache-based counters, bounded to a handful of requests per 1-hour window, and the limiter is explicitly fail-open by design (lines 52-56) -- leniency under contention is consistent with intent, not a regression.

Net: the headline per-IP bypass does not hold (skipped IP increments only occur on already-blocked no-op requests), and the residual race is an accepted low-impact fail-open pattern. False positive.

expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/tokens/create/ requires IsAuthenticated (JWT). Rate-limited by UserPATMintHourThrottle (10/hour per user). Accepts {name, scopes (optional, defaults to ['sessions:write','sessions:read']), expires_in_days (optional, 1-365)}. Validates scopes against ALLOWED_PAT_SCOPES — invalid scopes return 400. Generates a cryptographically random token (secrets.token_urlsafe(32) prefixed with 'pat_'), stores only the SHA-256 hash and first-8-char prefix. Returns {id, name, token (raw, shown ONCE), token_prefix, scopes, expires_at, created_at, warning} with 201. Raw token is never stored.
original_claim: When a client explicitly passes `scopes: []` (an empty list), `PATCreateView.post` evaluates `data.get('scopes') or list(DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES)` at line 73 — an empty list is falsy, so the default scopes are silently applied instead of honoring the caller's explicit empty-scope intent. The client receives a token with broader scopes than requested without any error or warning. The serializer's `validate_scopes` accepts an empty list as valid (no invalid entries), making the silent override inconsistent.
refutation: Mechanically the claim is accurate: `scopes = data.get('scopes') or list(DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES)` (pat_views.py:73) treats an explicit `[]` the same as an omitted field and applies the default scopes. But this is not a real user-facing defect. (1) The serializer declares `scopes` as `required=False, default=list` (pat_views.py:18), so by line 73 there is no way to distinguish "field omitted" from "explicit empty list" — both arrive as `[]`. The documented contract (FA-1064 expected_behaviour) states scopes is "optional, defaults to ['sessions:write','sessions:read']", i.e. absence -> defaults is intended, and the data model cannot tell the two cases apart. (2) ALLOWED_PAT_SCOPES contains exactly the two DEFAULT_PAT_SCOPES (permissions.py:12), so there is no narrower scope a caller could legitimately intend; honoring `[]` would only yield a functionally inert zero-privilege token (permissions.py:31 returns False for any required scope when scopes is empty), which no client or UI requests. (3) It is not an escalation/security issue: the caller is the authenticated owner and the grant is bounded to that user's own two scopes. No client, UI, or documented path sends `[]` deliberately, so no real subscriber experiences a deviation. This is intended/acceptable behavior driven by the serializer's `default=list`, not an exploitable or user-visible defect.

expected_behaviour: Wired via pre_save + @receiver(post_save) on Tenant in apps/tenants/agenda_signals.py. pre_save captures the prior welcomes_sent dict from DB into _PRE_SAVE_WELCOMES keyed by tenant PK. post_save diffs current vs prior: for each key that transitioned from absent/null to a timestamp, schedules mark_surfaced (kind=FEATURE_INTRO, item_id=feature, signal='welcome_delivered') + mark_state (state=COMPLETED) via transaction.on_commit. Idempotent: only transitions (null → timestamp) trigger the mirror; stable non-null values on subsequent saves are no-ops. Does not fire inside TestCase (on_commit deferred).
original_claim: In `_snapshot_welcomes_sent`, the `if not instance.pk:` branch stores the pre-save snapshot under the key `""` (empty string). However, for `post_save`, `pk = str(instance.pk)` is the real UUID string, so `_PRE_SAVE_WELCOMES.pop(pk, {})` misses the stored snapshot and falls back to `{}`. In practice this is harmless because a brand-new Tenant row always has `welcomes_sent = {}` (the field default), so `current == prior == {}` and no spurious transitions fire. But the dead-code path in `_snapshot_welcomes_sent` would cause agenda mirrors to fire incorrectly for any new tenant whose `welcomes_sent` was non-empty at creation, if that ever happens. With Django UUID PKs (`default=uuid.uuid4`), `instance.pk` is never None in pre_save anyway, making the branch permanently dead.
refutation: False positive. The QA correctly identifies that the `if not instance.pk:` branch (agenda_signals.py:44-47) stores its empty snapshot under key str(instance.pk or "") (i.e. ""), while post_save reads _PRE_SAVE_WELCOMES.pop(str(instance.pk), {}) -- a key mismatch. But this is harmless dead code with zero behavioral impact, for three independent reasons. (1) The branch is permanently dead: Tenant.id = UUIDField(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4) (models.py:141). Django evaluates field defaults at instance __init__, so instance.pk is always populated before save() runs; `not instance.pk` is never True for a normally-constructed Tenant. (2) Even if it fired, the fallback is identical: the branch caches {} ("new tenant -- treat all keys as newly set") and the post_save miss falls back to exactly {}. Same value either way, so prior == {} regardless, and the intended semantics (every welcomes_sent key on a brand-new row counts as a fresh transition) are preserved by the fallback. (3) No creation path sets welcomes_sent non-empty: the field is default=dict and grep found no Tenant.objects.create(welcomes_sent=...) or Tenant(welcomes_sent=...); it is only mutated via later .save(update_fields=["welcomes_sent", ...]) (runtime_views.py:85, plus fuel/core/finance views). So at creation current == {}, the transition loop (lines 78-84) yields zero transitions and post_save returns early -- no mirror fires. The QA's hypothesized harm (mirrors firing incorrectly for a new tenant with non-empty welcomes_sent) is both backwards (mirroring fresh keys on a genuinely new tenant is the intended behavior) and unreachable in practice. No user or operator can observe a wrong or missing AgendaEngagement mirror from this. At most it is a cosmetic dead-code cleanup, not a defect.
